detailed_results.title: AlphaEdit: Null-Space Constrained Knowledge Editing for Language Models

review.summary: AlphaEdit proposes projecting parameter perturbations onto the null space of preserved knowledge before applying them during locate-then-edit knowledge editing, theoretically guaranteeing that post-edited LLMs maintain unchanged outputs for preserved knowledge. The method is implemented as a single-line modification to existing methods and achieves an average 36.7% improvement across multiple LLMs and benchmarks.
strengths: Exceptional simplicity-to-performance ratio: a single line of code modification yields substantial improvements across multiple base methods (MEMIT, PRUNE, RECT), making adoption essentially frictionless.
Clean theoretical foundation: the null-space projection guarantee (ΔP·K₀ = 0) is exact for the estimated K₀, providing principled rather than heuristic preservation of existing knowledge.

Comprehensive experimental evaluation spanning three LLMs (GPT2-XL, GPT-J, LLaMA3-8B), two benchmarks, five editing-specific metrics, and six general capability tasks, with particularly convincing results in the challenging sequential editing setting.
Plug-and-play generality demonstrated across four different locate-then-edit methods, suggesting the principle operates at the paradigm level rather than being method-specific.
The hidden representation distributional analysis (Figure 5) provides compelling mechanistic evidence that AlphaEdit prevents the representational drift that degrades other methods.
weaknesses: Limited conceptual novelty: null-space projection for knowledge preservation is well-established in continual learning (Adam-NSCL, OWM). While the application to LLM knowledge editing is valuable, the core mathematical insight is borrowed.

The K₀ approximation using 100K Wikipedia samples is a significant practical heuristic whose impact on theoretical guarantees is underexplored—the guarantee is only as strong as this approximation, and the paper doesn't analyze how representative these samples are of the full preserved knowledge.

No analysis of null-space capacity limits: as sequential edits accumulate and the preserved knowledge matrix grows, the effective null space shrinks. The paper doesn't characterize when edit capacity saturates or how many edits can be sustained before the null space becomes too restrictive.
All experiments use models ≤8B parameters. Scalability to 70B+ models is unknown, and SVD computation on K₀K₀ᵀ may face numerical stability issues at larger scales.

The eigenvalue threshold of 10⁻² for determining 'non-zero' eigenvalues is a practical heuristic without principled justification. Sensitivity analysis on this critical hyperparameter appears limited.
review.justification: AlphaEdit delivers a well-executed application of null-space projection to LLM knowledge editing with strong empirical results and clean theoretical grounding. While the core technique is borrowed from continual learning (limiting novelty), the extraordinary practical utility (single-line code change, 36.7% average improvement, plug-and-play compatibility) and comprehensive evaluation across models, datasets, and metrics make this a solid contribution that meets the acceptance bar. The paper addresses a timely and important problem in sequential knowledge editing.

detailed_results.title: Identifying latent state transitions in non-linear dynamical systems

review.summary: This paper extends identifiable representation learning in dynamical systems by proving that not only the latent states but also the nonlinear transition function can be identified (up to permutation and element-wise invertible transformations). The key theoretical insight involves augmenting the transition function with process noise to achieve bijectivity, enabling nonlinear ICA-style identifiability proofs. A VAE-based practical algorithm is proposed and validated on synthetic, semi-realistic, and real-world dynamical systems.
strengths: First identifiability result for nonlinear transition functions in latent dynamical systems—a genuine and meaningful theoretical novelty that fills a clear gap in the literature
Elegant augmentation trick that transforms a non-bijective transition function into a bijective one, making the problem amenable to nonlinear ICA techniques
Tight theory-practice connection: the VAE-based algorithm faithfully encodes theoretical assumptions (conditionally independent neural spline flows, separate MLPs per component)
Comprehensive experimental evaluation across synthetic, cartpole, and MoCap data, with appropriate baselines from both identifiable representation learning and neural ODE/SDE communities
Compelling domain adaptation experiments (Section 4.3) that demonstrate the practical value of identifying dynamics, not just states, with only 50 sequences needed for transfer
The forward transition model enables direct future prediction, a practical advantage over prior identifiable methods that used inverse models
Clear discussion of assumptions with concrete examples (e.g., cartpole wind conditions) and honest acknowledgment of limitations
weaknesses: The bijectivity assumption (A1) on the augmented transition function is restrictive and excludes systems with intrinsic stochasticity in the controller or where the augmented map is not invertible—this is a meaningful constraint for many real-world systems
The requirement for regime annotations (auxiliary variable u set to regime labels) at training time is a practical limitation that restricts applicability to settings where such labels are available
No experiments with high-dimensional visual observations (e.g., actual video/pixel inputs), which would more convincingly demonstrate the claimed applicability to sensory measurements
The sufficient variability assumptions (A3, A4) lack practical verification methods—the paper provides no guidance on how to check these assumptions hold in a given dataset
Scalability to high-dimensional latent spaces (K >> 10) is not explored, leaving open questions about computational feasibility for complex systems
The MOCAP-SINGLE result (R=1, violating sufficient variability) performs well, which is encouraging but also raises questions about how tight the theoretical conditions actually are
No downstream control or reinforcement learning experiments to demonstrate the full potential of identifiable dynamics for decision-making
review.justification: The paper makes a genuine theoretical contribution by establishing the first identifiability result for nonlinear transition functions in latent dynamical systems, addressing a clear gap between state-level identifiability and practical needs for dynamics recovery. The proof technique is elegant and the practical algorithm maintains a tight theory-practice connection. While the restrictive bijectivity assumption and lack of pixel-level experiments are notable limitations, the comprehensive empirical validation—including strong domain adaptation and long-horizon prediction results—demonstrates meaningful practical value. This represents a solid contribution that will advance research in identifiable representation learning for dynamical systems.

detailed_results.title: PhysPDE: Rethinking PDE Discovery and a Physical Hypothesis Selection Benchmark

review.summary: PhysPDE reframes PDE discovery as a hypothesis selection problem within established physical frameworks, formulated as a bi-level nonlinear mixed-integer programming problem with decision forest encoding of physical constraints. The method uses an MCTS-inspired search (HSTS) for hypothesis selection and sequential threshold optimization (STOP) for inner symbolic regression, demonstrated on synthetic fluid mechanics and laser fusion datasets.
strengths: Novel and well-motivated paradigm shift from 'PDE discovery' to 'PDE interpretation/hypothesis selection' that better aligns with how physicists actually work—they need to determine which mechanism explains data, not rediscover known equations

Elegant mathematical formulation: the decision forest representation cleanly encodes mutual exclusivity, hierarchy, and conditional dependencies among physical hypotheses, and the bi-level optimization separates hypothesis selection from parameter recovery
Strong extrapolation performance compared to PDE-FIND (orders of magnitude better on test sets with different boundary conditions), highlighting the value of physics-constrained approaches
Practically relevant scientific application in laser fusion diagnostics where direct measurement is impossible, demonstrating genuine utility beyond toy problems
Clean benchmark contribution with two datasets of varying complexity and open-source release, providing a foundation for a new research direction
weaknesses: All experiments rely entirely on synthetic (COMSOL-simulated) data with relatively mild noise levels (1% multiplicative Gaussian); no real experimental data is used, significantly limiting claims about practical applicability

Baseline comparisons are limited and potentially unfair: PDE-FIND is from 2017, SGA-PDE and DISCOVER reportedly 'failed entirely' without sufficient investigation into whether this reflects fair implementation or inherent problem setup bias, and GPT-4o is not designed for this task
Scalability is unclear—the largest search space tested is ~3500 combinations for fluid mechanics and only 32 for laser fusion; real scientific problems may involve much larger hypothesis spaces
The method requires extensive domain expertise for constructing the hypothesis universe and decision forest structure, limiting automation and broader applicability across domains
The decision forest assumes single-parent dependency structures, which may be insufficient for more complex physical theories with multi-way interactions between hypotheses
Parameter estimation relies on SLSQP with physics-informed initialization, which may not generalize to domains where such initialization priors are unavailable
review.justification: The paper introduces a genuinely novel and well-motivated task paradigm that better aligns ML4Science with actual scientific practice, shifting from unconstrained PDE discovery to physically-constrained hypothesis selection. The mathematical formulation is clean and the benchmark contribution is valuable. However, the reliance on purely synthetic data, limited and potentially unfair baseline comparisons, and unclear scalability prevent this from being a strong accept. The conceptual contribution and practical relevance to the ML4Science community, particularly the laser fusion application, are sufficient for a poster-level acceptance.

detailed_results.title: ChartMimic: Evaluating LMM's Cross-Modal Reasoning Capability via Chart-to-Code Generation

review.summary: ChartMimic introduces a benchmark of 4,800 human-curated (figure, instruction, code) triplets for evaluating large multimodal models on chart-to-code generation, requiring cross-modal reasoning across visual understanding and code generation. The paper proposes multi-level evaluation metrics including a code-tracer approach and evaluates 17 models across two tasks (Direct Mimic and Customized Mimic).
strengths: Well-motivated problem at the intersection of visual understanding, code generation, and cross-modal reasoning, addressing a genuine gap between chart understanding and code generation benchmarks

The code-tracer evaluation methodology is methodologically clever, extracting structured information from code execution rather than relying on imperfect image-based comparison, yielding more reliable fine-grained metrics

Comprehensive evaluation of 17 models (3 proprietary, 14 open-weight) with multiple prompting strategies (HintEnhanced, SelfReflection, Scaffold) and detailed error analysis providing actionable diagnostic insights
Careful data curation pipeline with anti-leakage measures including temporal filtering (post-Feb 2024), color scheme replacement, and manual verification
Practical motivation rooted in a genuine scientific workflow (reproducing chart styles from papers), making the benchmark ecologically valid rather than artificial
Rich taxonomy covering 22 chart types (18 regular + 4 advanced) diversified into 201 subcategories, with well-designed complexity stratification
weaknesses: The 4,800 samples derive from approximately 600 seed prototypes through augmentation (3 augmented variants per seed), significantly inflating the apparent scale; the true diversity may be closer to 600 unique scenarios rather than 4,800
Python/Matplotlib-only focus limits generalizability to the broader visualization ecosystem (R/ggplot2, JavaScript/D3.js, Plotly, etc.) and constrains what the benchmark actually measures
Using GPT-4o as the high-level evaluator introduces circularity when evaluating GPT-4o itself and creates a dependency on a proprietary model that may change over time, undermining reproducibility
Moderate human correlation (Pearson r = 0.70 for high-level, 0.77 for low-level) indicates non-trivial disagreement with human judgment, suggesting the metrics may miss important quality dimensions
The contribution is primarily a benchmark without new methods or algorithmic innovations; the technical novelty beyond careful curation and the code-tracer metric is limited
Limited systematic analysis of which specific chart types or complexity levels drive the performance gaps—aggregate statistics dominate over fine-grained per-category insights
review.justification: ChartMimic is a well-constructed benchmark that fills a genuine gap in LMM evaluation by combining visual understanding and code generation in a practically motivated task. The code-tracer evaluation methodology and comprehensive model evaluation are clear strengths. However, the effective diversity is limited by heavy augmentation from ~600 seeds, the single-library focus constrains generalizability, and the contribution is primarily a benchmark without algorithmic novelty. It meets the acceptance bar as a solid resource contribution but does not reach the level of exceptional impact.

detailed_results.title: Partial Gromov-Wasserstein Metric

review.summary: This paper proposes the Partial Gromov-Wasserstein (PGW) problem, a specific instance of the Unbalanced GW framework using total variation penalties instead of KL-divergence, and rigorously proves it defines a proper metric between general (non-probability) metric measure spaces. The authors provide two equivalent Frank-Wolfe solvers, introduce PGW barycenters, and demonstrate effectiveness on shape matching, retrieval, and interpolation tasks.
strengths: The core theoretical contribution—proving PGW is a metric on mm-spaces—fills a genuine and important gap in the optimal transport literature, as existing UGW formulations only yield discrepancies without metric guarantees.
The triangle inequality proof via Caffarelli-McCann augmentation is elegant, bridging PGW to well-studied GW theory in a principled way.
Comprehensive theoretical treatment including existence of minimizers, consistency with GW as λ→∞, and formal convergence analysis of the solvers.
The two equivalent Frank-Wolfe solvers provide both theoretical insight and practical flexibility, with the equivalence proof being a nice result.
PGW is computationally much faster than UGW (35.92s vs 1484.49s on Dataset I) while achieving competitive or superior performance, demonstrating practical viability.
Code is publicly available, supporting reproducibility.
weaknesses: Experiments are limited to small-scale point clouds (hundreds of points); scalability to thousands or millions of points—relevant for many real-world applications—remains untested.

Only shape analysis applications are explored, despite GW having significant traction in graph learning, single-cell genomics, and NLP; broader application domains would substantially strengthen the practical impact claim.
The gap between theory and practice regarding non-convexity is insufficiently addressed: metric properties hold for global optima, but the solvers only guarantee convergence to stationary points.

Parameter selection for λ lacks systematic guidance; the paper shows sensitivity to this parameter but does not provide principled selection strategies beyond cross-validation or domain-specific heuristics.

The comparison with MPGW (Chapel et al., 2020) reveals inconsistent advantages—MPGW performs well in matching but poorly in retrieval—without sufficient explanation of when each formulation should be preferred.
review.justification: The paper makes a clear and rigorous theoretical contribution by establishing PGW as the first proper metric between non-probability mm-spaces in the GW framework, which fills a genuine gap. The proofs are elegant and the algorithmic development is sound. However, the experimental evaluation is limited in scale and application breadth, and the practical implications of the theory-practice gap (global vs local optima) deserve more attention. Overall, this is a solid contribution that meets the acceptance bar through its theoretical novelty and completeness.

detailed_results.title: Chemistry-Inspired Diffusion with Non-Differentiable Guidance

review.summary: The paper proposes ChemGuide, a method that uses non-differentiable quantum chemistry oracles (GFN2-xTB) to guide unconditional diffusion models for 3D molecular generation via Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation (SPSA), eliminating the need for large labeled datasets or differentiable property predictors. The approach is formulated as a bilevel optimization problem that can be composed with existing explicit and implicit guidance methods for joint property and stability optimization.
strengths: Clean and well-motivated integration of quantum chemistry with diffusion models; the use of SPSA to bridge non-differentiable oracles with gradient-guided diffusion is elegant and practically important

Careful attention to physical constraints: preserving equivariance through zero-mean perturbations and restricting guidance to atomic positions (not atom types) demonstrates genuine domain understanding
The bilevel optimization framework cleanly separates property optimization from stability optimization and is composable with existing guidance approaches (both explicit and implicit)
DFT-level validation (Table 3) demonstrates that improvements from the cheaper GFN2-xTB oracle transfer to higher-fidelity evaluation, which is critical for practical credibility

The method generalizes across datasets (QM9, GEOM), molecule sizes, and properties (stability, HOMO-LUMO gap, polarizability), with stronger gains on larger molecules where neural approximations are less reliable
The framework has broad potential beyond chemistry—any domain with accurate non-differentiable simulators could benefit from analogous approaches
weaknesses: Improvements are sometimes modest, particularly validity gains on QM9 (1.6%) which may not be practically significant; the force reductions, while more substantial (up to 14%), need more context on whether they are chemically meaningful
Sample sizes for evaluation are small (200-500 molecules), making it difficult to assess statistical significance of improvements; no confidence intervals or statistical tests are reported

Computational overhead is substantial (800 xTB calculations per molecule for 400 guided steps) and may limit practical adoption, especially for larger molecules or higher-fidelity oracles; no wall-clock time comparisons are provided
No formal analysis of SPSA estimator variance in high-dimensional molecular spaces; the sensitivity to the perturbation parameter c and guidance scale s is acknowledged but not thoroughly analyzed

Missing comparison with other zeroth-order optimization methods (e.g., finite differences with equivariance-preserving projections, evolutionary strategies) that could serve as alternative gradient estimators
The guidance scale selection from a discrete set is manual and property-dependent, which limits plug-and-play applicability
review.justification: The paper presents a clean and physically well-motivated framework for integrating non-differentiable quantum chemistry oracles with diffusion-based molecular generation. The SPSA-based guidance with careful equivariance preservation is a novel and generalizable contribution. However, the experimental improvements are sometimes modest, sample sizes are small without statistical significance testing, and computational costs are substantial. The work opens an interesting research direction and meets the acceptance bar as a solid poster contribution, though stronger experimental validation and comparisons would have strengthened it.

detailed_results.title: Planning Anything with Rigor: General-Purpose Zero-Shot Planning with LLM-based Formalized Programming

review.summary: LLMFP proposes a general-purpose zero-shot planning framework that uses LLMs to formalize natural language planning problems as constrained optimization problems (solved via SMT solvers) through a multi-stage pipeline (Definer, Formulator, Code Generator, Result Formatter, Self-Assessment), achieving strong performance across 9 diverse planning domains without task-specific examples.
strengths: Genuine generality demonstrated across 9 diverse planning domains (multi-constraint and multi-step) without any task-specific in-context examples, PDDL domain files, or pre-defined critics
Strong empirical results with 83.7% (GPT-4o) and 86.8% (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) optimal rates, representing 37-40% improvements over the strongest baseline (o1-preview direct planning)

The Mystery Blocksworld experiment cleverly demonstrates that the framework encodes problem structure rather than relying on semantic shortcuts, with strong 77.7-98.0% performance despite obfuscated predicate names
Comprehensive ablation studies systematically validate each component's contribution, and the multi-stage decomposition provides interpretability and debuggability
The principled paradigm of 'LLM as formalizer + solver as executor' addresses well-documented LLM limitations in constraint satisfaction and multi-step reasoning
Testing across multiple frontier LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) demonstrates framework robustness
weaknesses: Scalability is entirely untested - all benchmark problems are small-scale, and SMT/MILP solver performance can degrade dramatically on industrial-sized problems with thousands of variables, which is critical for claimed applicability to supply chain and logistics

The 'zero-shot' claim is somewhat misleading: the Formulator stage uses two task-agnostic examples encoding non-trivial structural knowledge about JSON decomposition, and these examples represent careful design effort

The comparison with o1-preview is incomplete - o1-preview was only used for the Direct baseline, but testing it with Code SMT or other structured approaches would significantly strengthen the comparison and better isolate the contribution

The framework is fundamentally limited to deterministic, fully observable planning problems expressible as constrained optimization, excluding stochastic, partially observable, and adversarial settings - this significantly narrows the generality claim
Dependence on proprietary frontier LLMs with API-level access limits reproducibility; even with temperature 0, non-determinism exists across API versions

Sensitivity analysis for key hyperparameters (5-iteration self-assessment cap, 5-attempt code regeneration cap) is absent, and the cost implications of multiple LLM calls are addressed only briefly in the appendix

No comparison with classical planning systems (e.g., Fast Downward with automatically generated PDDL) or established optimization modeling tools - this would better contextualize where the approach sits in the broader landscape
review.justification: The paper presents a well-executed framework that demonstrates a principled and practical approach to LLM-based planning through formalization rather than direct generation. The breadth of evaluation across 9 diverse tasks, strong empirical gains over baselines (including o1-preview), clever experimental design (Mystery Blocksworld), and thorough ablations provide solid support for the claims. However, the novelty is moderate (combining existing capabilities—LLM code generation, multi-stage prompting, SMT solvers—in a pipeline), scalability to realistic problem sizes is unvalidated, and the problem scope limitation to deterministic/fully-observable settings tempers the generality claims. On balance, the practical utility, empirical strength, and the paradigm's potential to influence future neurosymbolic planning work merit acceptance.

detailed_results.title: On the Benefits of Memory for Modeling Time-Dependent PDEs

review.summary: This paper investigates the benefits of incorporating memory (past states) into neural PDE solvers. Grounded in the Mori-Zwanzig formalism, the authors theoretically show that memory can be arbitrarily more important than Markovian dynamics under partial observability, and introduce MemNO (combining S4 with FNO) to demonstrate up to 6× error reduction at low resolutions or under observation noise.
strengths: Clear identification of *when* memory helps: partial observability (low resolution, noise) combined with significant high-frequency content. This resolves an apparent contradiction with prior work claiming Markovian approaches are sufficient.

Well-structured experimental design across multiple PDEs (KS, NS, Burgers), resolutions, viscosities, and noise levels, with compelling identification of a 'cutoff resolution' below which memory becomes crucial.

The Mori-Zwanzig theoretical motivation is cleanly articulated and directly informs the architecture design; the ωf metric (fraction of unobserved modes) provides a practical diagnostic for practitioners.
Graceful degradation property: MemNO matches Markovian performance at high resolution while dramatically improving at low resolution, meaning memory doesn't hurt when unnecessary.
Modular framework demonstrated with multiple combinations (S4+FFNO, LSTM+FFNO, S4+U-Net), making the approach broadly applicable beyond the specific architecture choices.
Valuable benchmark contribution: new challenging KS datasets with low viscosity address the observation that existing benchmarks predominantly feature solutions with weak high-frequency content.
weaknesses: The theoretical example (Theorem 1) involves a specifically constructed linear PDE with exponentially growing solutions (eigenvalues of order 2^B), limiting its direct applicability to physically relevant systems. The gap between this theoretical result and the nonlinear experimental settings could be tighter.
Experiments are limited to 1D and 2D PDEs; scalability to 3D problems—both in terms of memory benefits and computational overhead—remains unclear.
The noise experiments use only i.i.d. Gaussian noise; more realistic noise models (correlated noise, aliasing artifacts, structured measurement errors) would strengthen the practical claims.
The architectural contribution (combining S4 with FNO) is relatively straightforward; the paper acknowledges that systematic comparison between different memory architectures is left as future work.
Limited computational cost analysis: while S4 is efficient, maintaining memory during long rollouts has overhead that is not thoroughly characterized, particularly relative to the error improvements.

The connection between the linear theoretical example and the nonlinear experimental phenomena (turbulence, chaos) relies heavily on the general Mori-Zwanzig framework rather than specific analysis, leaving the theoretical contribution somewhat disconnected from practice.
review.justification: The paper makes a meaningful contribution by clearly characterizing when memory benefits neural PDE solvers, resolving an apparent misconception in the community that Markovian approaches are universally sufficient. The combination of Mori-Zwanzig-motivated theory, a modular architecture, comprehensive experiments, and new challenging benchmarks constitutes a solid contribution. The main limitations—narrow theoretical example, limited scalability demonstration, and straightforward architecture—prevent a higher rating, but the practical insights and empirical evidence are compelling enough for acceptance.

detailed_results.title: A Generic Framework for Conformal Fairness

review.summary: This paper formalizes 'Conformal Fairness' by adapting classical fairness metrics (demographic parity, equal opportunity, equalized odds, predictive equality, predictive parity) from point predictions to conformal prediction sets, and provides a theoretically grounded algorithm to control coverage gaps between sensitive groups. The framework extends to non-IID settings (graph data) via exchangeability and requires no group information at inference time.
strengths: Comprehensive framework unifying multiple fairness metrics under a single conformal prediction umbrella, with clean theoretical composition of lemmas supporting the overall algorithm
No group information required at inference time — the framework calibrates offline and applies a single optimal threshold, which is a critical practical advantage over competitors
Extension to graph data via the exchangeability assumption (rather than IID) is a meaningful contribution that addresses an underexplored area in fair graph neural networks

Thorough experimental evaluation across five datasets (graph and tabular), five non-conformity scores, four fairness metrics, and multiple closeness thresholds, with honest acknowledgment of limitations in intersectional settings
Practical applicability to regulatory compliance (Four-Fifths Rule integration) and black-box fairness auditing of existing conformal predictors

Theorem 3.4 connecting predictive parity feasibility to total variation distance between group-conditioned label distributions is technically interesting, and the Predictive Parity Proxy is a practical workaround
weaknesses: Individual theoretical contributions (Lemmas 3.1-3.3) are relatively straightforward extensions of existing CP theory — the novelty lies more in the systematic composition than in technical depth

Limited comparison with existing fairness-aware CP methods: the BatchGCP comparison is relegated to the appendix, and no head-to-head comparison with recent conditional coverage methods or other fair CP approaches (e.g., Romano et al. group-conditional CP) is provided in the main text
The discrete search over the threshold space Λ lacks discussion of computational complexity, convergence properties, or sensitivity to grid resolution — this could matter in practice
Classification only: the framework does not address regression tasks, which significantly limits scope given that much of the fairness literature addresses continuous outcomes

The fundamental small-group problem (finite-sample slack of 1/(|D_calib(g,y)|+1)) is acknowledged but not adequately addressed — no formal guidance on minimum calibration sizes is provided, and this substantially undermines intersectional fairness guarantees
Efficiency costs can be substantial (e.g., prediction set sizes increasing from ~2.2 to ~2.7 on ACSIncome) but the paper lacks systematic analysis of the fairness-efficiency Pareto frontier
review.justification: The paper provides a well-executed and comprehensive framework connecting conformal prediction with multiple fairness notions, filling a clear gap in the literature. The extension to graph data and inference-time group-agnosticism are practical differentiators. However, the theoretical contributions are incremental extensions of standard CP theory rather than deeply novel, the comparison with existing methods is limited, and the classification-only scope constrains impact. The work is borderline but the breadth, practical relevance, and clean presentation tip it toward acceptance as a poster.

detailed_results.title: Long-time asymptotics of noisy SVGD outside the population limit

review.summary: This paper studies the long-time asymptotic behavior of Noisy SVGD (NSVGD) in the finite-particle regime, establishing that the limit set as iterations k→∞ is well-defined, converges to the target distribution as n→∞, and that these limits are interchangeable. The approach leverages a novel connection between the discrete NSVGD iterates and McKean-Vlasov processes, resolving the variance collapse issue of deterministic SVGD.
strengths: Addresses a fundamental and well-posed open question about SVGD's behavior in the practically relevant finite-particle, infinite-time regime, which prior work could not characterize

Elegant and rigorous proof strategy connecting discrete-time particle dynamics to McKean-Vlasov theory, with clear intermediate results (stability, interpolation, KL dissipation) that are of independent interest
The interchangeability of limits (n→∞ and k→∞) is a clean and satisfying theoretical result that provides conceptual clarity about the algorithm
Provides a principled explanation for why deterministic SVGD suffers variance collapse (Dirac measures are stationary) and a minimal algorithmic fix (noise injection) with theoretical guarantees
Well-motivated and well-placed in the literature, with clear exposition of the gap between existing population-limit results and finite-particle behavior
Proposition 3 establishing that NSVGD trajectories approximate McKean-Vlasov processes is a result of independent interest applicable beyond SVGD
weaknesses: Results are purely qualitative with no convergence rates: neither the rate at which L^n approaches π as n→∞ nor the rate of convergence to L^n as k→∞ are provided, significantly limiting practical utility
Results are not uniform in dimension d, which is a critical gap since variance collapse is most problematic in high dimensions—the regime where theoretical guarantees are most needed

Very limited experimental validation: only Gaussian targets are tested, which are among the simplest distributions; experiments on multimodal, heavy-tailed, or high-dimensional targets would substantially strengthen claims
No guidance on selecting the regularization parameter λ, which trades off between SVGD's transport behavior and noise-induced mixing; this is left entirely to future work

The cleaner non-averaged result (Theorem 2) requires the stronger Log-Sobolev inequality assumption, while the averaged result (Theorem 1) yields a weaker convergence guarantee (Cesàro average rather than pointwise)

The connection to practice is somewhat tenuous: while NSVGD is simple to implement, without quantitative rates or dimension-dependent analysis, it is unclear when the theoretical guarantees become operative
review.justification: The paper makes a genuine theoretical contribution by being the first to characterize the long-time behavior of SVGD-type algorithms in the finite-particle regime, resolving an important open question with an elegant McKean-Vlasov framework. However, the purely qualitative (non-quantitative) nature of the convergence results, the lack of dimension uniformity, and limited experimental scope reduce the practical impact. The work is at the acceptance bar as a solid theoretical contribution to the sampling and interacting particle systems literature.

detailed_results.title: Approximation algorithms for combinatorial optimization with predictions

review.summary: The paper proposes a generic black-box method for using predictions to improve approximation guarantees of combinatorial optimization algorithms for selection problems (choosing a feasible subset of items of optimal total weight). The core technique—zeroing out weights of predicted items for minimization (or non-predicted for maximization) before running a standard ρ-approximation algorithm—yields smooth interpolation between optimal (perfect predictions) and ρ-approximate (no useful predictions), with matching UGC-based lower bounds and a refined problem-specific algorithm for Steiner Tree.
strengths: Exceptional generality: a single elegant framework covers Vertex Cover, Steiner Tree, Min-Weight Perfect Matching, Knapsack, Maximum Clique, and many more, while preserving near-linear running times.

Matching lower bounds: UGC-based impossibility results show the black-box approach is optimal for the class of selection problems as a whole, providing a rare tight connection between learning-augmented algorithm design and computational complexity.
The refined Steiner Tree algorithm (Section 4) demonstrates genuine algorithmic depth beyond the black-box framework, using a scaling parameter α to cap false positive costs via MST connections.
Handles infeasible predictions naturally—a practically crucial feature since producing feasible predictions is itself computationally hard for most problems.
Clean, well-organized writing with clearly stated results and accessible proofs; the framework is immediately implementable.
weaknesses: The core technique (zeroing out predicted/non-predicted item weights) is conceptually very simple and arguably the first thing one would try, limiting the perceived technical depth of the main contribution.

Experimental evaluation is narrow: only Steiner Tree is tested empirically, despite the paper claiming applicability to many problems. Broader experiments (e.g., Vertex Cover, Knapsack, Matching) would substantially strengthen the practical case.

The approximation ratio 1 + (η⁺ + (ρ-1)η⁻)/OPT can be vacuous when prediction error is comparable to OPT; the robustification via parallel execution of the conventional algorithm is a standard fix but not deeply satisfying.
Only one problem (Steiner Tree) receives a refined problem-specific treatment; the extent to which similar refinements are possible for other problems remains entirely open.

The computational cost of generating good predictions is externalized. For the framework to be practical, amortization over many similar instances is required, but this is not analyzed beyond brief discussion.
review.justification: The paper makes a clean and broadly applicable theoretical contribution: a simple but provably optimal black-box framework for learning-augmented approximation algorithms, backed by matching UGC-based lower bounds. The Steiner Tree refinement adds meaningful depth. However, the core technique's simplicity limits the technical novelty, and the experimental evaluation is too narrow for the scope of claims made. Overall, it is a solid contribution that opens a systematic new direction, meeting the acceptance bar for a poster.

detailed_results.title: Spider 2.0: Evaluating Language Models on Real-World Enterprise Text-to-SQL Workflows

review.summary: Spider 2.0 introduces a benchmark of 632 real-world enterprise text-to-SQL workflow problems spanning multiple database systems (BigQuery, Snowflake, SQLite, DuckDB, PostgreSQL, ClickHouse), featuring complex schemas (avg. 812 columns), diverse SQL dialects, project-level codebases, and multi-step workflows. The best code agent (o1-preview) achieves only 21.3% on Spider 2.0 versus 91.2% on Spider 1.0, revealing a massive gap between current LLM capabilities and enterprise requirements.
strengths: Unprecedented realism: databases sourced from real enterprise applications with genuinely complex schemas, diverse SQL dialects, and multi-step workflows—a dramatic step up from prior benchmarks
Tiered evaluation design (Spider 2.0 agent, 2.0-lite, 2.0-snow) serves multiple research communities and paradigms, from traditional text-to-SQL to agentic code generation
Thorough construction methodology with a six-step pipeline, surface+semantic SQL rewriting to prevent data leakage, and multi-round quality control (45% error rate in round 1 → 5% in round 2)
Execution-based focused evaluation with custom per-example scripts is more robust than string/exact-match, and the red-team assessment adds further credibility

Comprehensive baseline evaluation across multiple frontier LLMs and agent frameworks with detailed error taxonomy providing actionable research directions (schema linking, dialect functions, query planning, nested schemas)
Extremely timely contribution: LLMs are being deployed for enterprise data analytics, yet existing benchmarks are near-saturated and bear little resemblance to real enterprise environments
weaknesses: 632 examples is modest for a benchmark, especially when subdivided by database type, difficulty level, and task features—statistical power for fine-grained analyses is limited

Cloud dependency (BigQuery, Snowflake) creates significant access barriers, reproducibility concerns as cloud data/schemas may evolve, and substantial evaluation costs that limit accessibility for resource-constrained researchers
Test-only benchmark without training data limits fine-tuning approaches and makes it harder for researchers to develop supervised methods specifically targeting enterprise SQL challenges
The difficulty classification by SQL token count is acknowledged as imperfect and somewhat coarse—a more nuanced difficulty taxonomy would strengthen the benchmark's analytical utility
Some ambiguity in what constitutes a correct answer for complex enterprise workflows, despite quality control; the paper could better discuss inter-annotator agreement or edge cases in evaluation
review.justification: Spider 2.0 is a landmark benchmark contribution that convincingly demonstrates the enormous gap between current text-to-SQL benchmarks and real-world enterprise requirements. The dramatic performance drop from 91.2% (Spider 1.0) to 21.3% (Spider 2.0) is a compelling and timely finding that will redirect research efforts. The construction methodology is rigorous, the evaluation design is thoughtful with three tiers and custom execution-based scripts, and the error analysis provides actionable research directions. While weaknesses exist (modest scale, cloud dependency, cost), these are practical rather than fundamental, and the benchmark's significance to both the NLP and data engineering communities warrants spotlight-level recognition.

detailed_results.title: Turning Up the Heat: Min-p Sampling for Creative and Coherent LLM Outputs

review.summary: The paper proposes min-p sampling, a simple truncation method for LLM text generation that dynamically adjusts the sampling threshold by retaining tokens with probability exceeding p_base × p_max (the maximum token probability). The method is evaluated across reasoning and creative writing benchmarks, multiple model families and scales, and a wide temperature range, showing particular improvements at high temperatures.
strengths: Extreme simplicity of the method—a one-line code change—makes it immediately practical and easy to adopt, as evidenced by integration into Hugging Face Transformers, vLLM, SGLang, and other major frameworks

Comprehensive experimental evaluation spanning multiple benchmarks (GPQA, GSM8K, AlpacaEval), model families (Mistral, Llama 3), model sizes (1B to 123B), and a wide temperature range (0.5–5.0), providing a thorough empirical picture
The Pareto frontier analysis comparing accuracy-diversity tradeoffs across 20 hyperparameter configurations per method is a more nuanced evaluation methodology than single-point comparisons
Human evaluation with attention checks and anti-AI filtering adds ecological validity beyond automated metrics
Clear and well-motivated intuition: the dynamic threshold scales naturally with model confidence, narrowing the candidate pool when confident and widening it when uncertain
weaknesses: No theoretical analysis of why linear scaling in p_max is the right functional form, or how min-p affects properties like entropy, perplexity, or distributional characteristics of generated text compared to alternatives—the contribution is purely empirical and heuristic

Performance improvements at standard temperatures (τ ≤ 1.0) are within reported standard error margins (±1–1.5%), making it difficult to claim statistical significance for the majority of practical use cases

Incomplete comparison with entropy-based adaptive methods (η and ε sampling) due to computational constraints and infrastructure differences (vLLM vs. Hugging Face), leaving a significant gap in understanding min-p's position in the landscape of adaptive sampling methods

Human evaluation scale is limited: 54 participants rating only three samples per configuration across 12 conditions provides limited statistical power, acknowledged by the authors as a 'point estimate'

The method's advantage is concentrated in the high-temperature regime (τ > 1.5), which represents a subset of typical use cases; the creative writing evaluation relies on LLM-as-judge (GPT-4 Turbo) with known systematic biases

The core idea originated from the open-source community rather than from principled analysis, and the paper primarily serves as empirical validation of an existing heuristic rather than presenting a genuinely new scientific insight
review.justification: While min-p sampling has demonstrated clear practical value through widespread adoption in open-source frameworks, the paper's academic contribution is insufficient for ICLR. The method is an intuitive but theoretically unjustified one-line heuristic with no analysis of why linear scaling in p_max is optimal. Performance improvements are marginal and statistically insignificant at standard temperatures, with the advantage concentrated in high-temperature regimes. Incomplete baselines against other adaptive methods (η, ε sampling) and limited human evaluation scale further weaken the empirical claims. The paper is better characterized as a well-executed engineering validation of a community-originated heuristic rather than a scientific advance.

detailed_results.title: Mini-batch Coresets for Memory-efficient Language Model Training on Data Mixtures

review.summary: The paper proposes CoLM, a method for selecting small mini-batch coresets that approximate larger mini-batch gradients during LLM training. It addresses three challenges: handling highly imbalanced data mixtures (by including all small-source examples), compatibility with Adam optimizer (via gradient normalization by historical exponential moving averages), and high dimensionality (via zeroth-order gradient estimation and sparsification of the last V-projection matrix).
strengths: Well-motivated theoretical analysis (Theorems 4.1-4.2) showing that gradient-matching coresets systematically exclude small data sources, leading to a principled and simple fix of retaining all small-source examples

Comprehensive experimental evaluation across four model families (Phi-2, Phi-3, Zephyr, Llama-3), two benchmark suites (MathInstruct, SuperGLUE), and seven baselines, with consistent improvements showing CoLM at bs=64 outperforms standard training at bs=256
Composability with existing memory-efficient methods (LoRA, gradient accumulation, quantization) since CoLM targets activation memory, an orthogonal axis to weight/optimizer memory reduction

Thorough ablation studies systematically validating each component: sparsification criteria, dimensionality choices, layer selection, gradient approximation methods, and the contribution of each design decision
The variance reduction theorem (4.3) provides a clean connection between coreset quality and convergence guarantees via standard SGD convergence bounds
weaknesses: Strong dependency on knowing or discovering data sources: the clustering-based approach for unlabeled data (used for SuperGLUE) is only briefly discussed and its robustness to clustering quality is not analyzed
Pre-training evaluation—where memory savings matter most due to larger batch sizes—is deferred to the appendix and appears limited in scope, undermining claims about broader applicability

The overhead of coreset selection (two forward passes for SPSA plus greedy submodular optimization with quadratic complexity in batch size) is not thoroughly analyzed, particularly for very large-scale settings

Narrow task evaluation focused on mathematical reasoning and classification; it remains unclear how well the method generalizes to other fine-tuning scenarios like creative generation, summarization, or multi-turn dialogue

The zeroth-order SPSA gradient estimation introduces a hyperparameter (perturbation scale ε) and approximation quality that are not thoroughly characterized in terms of sensitivity or theoretical error bounds
review.justification: The paper presents a principled approach to memory-efficient LLM training through mini-batch coresets, with solid theoretical motivation for each design choice and consistent empirical improvements across multiple models and benchmarks. The treatment of imbalanced data mixtures is the strongest novel contribution. However, the dependency on source identification, limited pre-training evaluation, scalability concerns at very large scales, and narrow task diversity prevent a stronger recommendation. The paper makes a sufficient contribution to warrant acceptance but falls short of being a spotlight-level paper.

detailed_results.title: PABBO: Preferential Amortized Black-Box Optimization

review.summary: PABBO introduces the first fully amortized framework for Preferential Bayesian Optimization (PBO), using a transformer-based neural process architecture trained via reinforcement learning with auxiliary losses to meta-learn both the surrogate model and acquisition function from pairwise preference feedback, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup over GP-based PBO methods while maintaining competitive or superior accuracy.
strengths: Addresses a genuine and well-articulated computational bottleneck: standard GP-based PBO requires expensive non-conjugate inference at each step, making it incompatible with real-time human interaction. The proposed amortization achieves 10-100× speedup by reducing inference to a single forward pass.

The architectural design is well-motivated: the transformer neural process naturally handles permutation-invariant preference histories, the data embedder separately encodes pair elements plus preference labels, and the self-attention masking strategy is appropriately adapted for the preferential setting.

The combination of RL-based policy learning with an auxiliary BCE prediction loss is a clever solution to reward sparsity, and the warm-up training phase is a pragmatic design choice that is well-justified.

Comprehensive experimental evaluation covering in-distribution GP samples, out-of-distribution test functions, HPO-B hyperparameter optimization, and real-world human preference datasets (Candy, Sushi), with five GP-based baselines, 30 random seeds, and confidence intervals.
Thorough ablation studies systematically examining reward type (value-based vs. ranking-based), discount factor, and query set size, demonstrating robustness of key design choices.
weaknesses: Scalability is a significant concern: experiments are limited to ≤4D problems, the O(S²) candidate pair evaluation grows quickly with dimensionality, and dimension-specific models are required, limiting practical deployment flexibility.

Despite targeting human-in-the-loop optimization as the primary motivation, no actual human experiments were conducted—all preferences are simulated, which weakens the practical validation of the claimed real-time interaction benefits.

Pre-training data dependence on GP-drawn samples augmented with quadratic bowls is somewhat ad hoc. While OOD experiments partially address this, generalization to functions with fundamentally different structure (e.g., highly multimodal, discontinuous) remains uncertain.

The conceptual contribution, while requiring non-trivial architectural and training adaptations, is a natural extension of existing amortized BO methods (NAP, PFNs) to the preferential setting, which limits the novelty somewhat.

Missing comparisons with other potential approaches: Bayesian neural network-based PBO methods, or adaptations of existing amortized BO methods to handle preferences, would strengthen the empirical positioning.
review.justification: PABBO makes a solid and timely contribution by filling a clear gap—amortizing PBO for real-time preference learning—with a well-designed architecture and training procedure that achieves impressive computational speedups while maintaining competitive accuracy. The experimental evaluation is thorough within the tested regime. However, the limited scalability (≤4D), lack of actual human experiments despite the human-in-the-loop motivation, and the somewhat incremental nature of extending amortized BO to preferences prevent this from being a strong accept. It meets the acceptance bar as a useful contribution to a growing subfield.

detailed_results.title: TorchTitan: One-stop PyTorch native solution for production ready LLM pretraining

review.summary: TorchTitan is a PyTorch-native distributed training system that unifies 4D parallelism (Data, Tensor, Pipeline, Context Parallel) into a composable, modular framework for LLM pretraining, built on DTensor and DeviceMesh abstractions. The system integrates production features (distributed checkpointing, Float8 training, debugging tools) and demonstrates accelerations of 30-65% over optimized baselines on Llama 3.1 models (8B-405B) across up to 512 H100 GPUs.
strengths: Addresses a genuine and pressing engineering pain point: the fragmentation and poor interoperability of distributed LLM training solutions. The unified design via DTensor and DeviceMesh is architecturally principled.

The progressive stacking evaluation methodology is sound—each optimization is measured incrementally against a baseline that includes all prior optimizations, isolating individual contributions effectively.

Comprehensive production engineering features (DCP with async checkpointing achieving 5-15x overhead reduction, Flight Recorder debugging, meta device initialization) demonstrate attention to real deployment concerns beyond just raw throughput.

Well-written paper with clear exposition of the system architecture, design decisions, and practical training recipes. The actionable guidance on selecting parallelism configurations fills a knowledge gap.

Open-source and PyTorch-native positioning maximizes potential for community adoption and ecosystem-wide impact. The connection to official Llama 3.1 training recipes provides immediate practical relevance.
Loss convergence validation across parallelism configurations confirms numerical correctness, which is important for a systems paper claiming production readiness.
weaknesses: Limited scientific novelty: all individual techniques (FSDP, TP, PP, CP, activation checkpointing, Float8, torch.compile) are pre-existing. The contribution is primarily in their integration and unification, which is valuable engineering but modest in terms of new algorithmic or scientific insight.

No direct head-to-head performance comparison with Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed, or other competing systems on identical hardware. Baselines are self-comparisons (FSDP without optimizations). The paper acknowledges this difficulty but the gap significantly weakens the claimed advantages.

Scale ceiling of 512 GPUs is insufficient to validate 'production ready' claims. Production LLM training (including Llama 3.1 itself) operates at 16K+ GPUs, where failure rates, network congestion, and load imbalance present qualitatively different challenges.

Experiments use 'non-standard' H100 GPUs (HBM2e, lower TDP), making results difficult to reproduce or compare with published benchmarks. MFU is not reported with Float8 enabled, further limiting comparability.
Evaluation is limited to the Llama architecture family. Claims of architecture-agnostic design are not empirically validated for mixture-of-experts, multimodal, or encoder-decoder architectures.
The reported MFU of 33-42% (without Float8) is moderate and not contextualized against what other systems achieve on comparable hardware, making it hard to assess absolute efficiency.
review.justification: While TorchTitan addresses a real engineering need and is well-executed as a systems project, its contribution is primarily in integrating existing techniques rather than introducing new scientific ideas. The absence of direct comparisons with competing systems (Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed), the limited evaluation scale (512 GPUs vs. claimed production readiness), and the restriction to a single model architecture weaken the empirical claims. For ICLR, where scientific novelty is a key criterion, this paper falls short despite its significant practical value—it would be better suited for a systems venue like MLSys or OSDI.

detailed_results.title: ProtComposer: Compositional Protein Structure Generation with 3D Ellipsoids

review.summary: ProtComposer introduces a compositional conditioning framework for protein structure generation using 3D ellipsoids as intermediate representations that capture substructure shape, position, orientation, and secondary structure type. The system fine-tunes Multiflow with a novel SE(3)-equivariant Invariant Cross Attention mechanism and demonstrates three inference modes (hand-specified, data-derived, and statistical model-based ellipsoids) that enable spatial layout control, structural editing, and improved generation diversity.
strengths: Novel and well-motivated representation: 3D ellipsoids provide a 'Goldilocks' level of abstraction for conditioning protein generation, drawing a compelling parallel to bounding-box/blob conditioning in image generation while addressing unique SE(3) equivariance requirements.

Strong technical design: The Invariant Cross Attention mechanism is principled and SE(3)-equivariant, with the zero-initialization design ensuring backward compatibility with the pretrained Multiflow model—enabling effective fine-tuning rather than training from scratch.

Genuinely new capabilities: The three inference modes (manual specification, data-derived editing, statistical model) each unlock distinct and previously unavailable functionalities, particularly the structural editing demonstrated in rotating helices, translating sheets, and inverting secondary structure.

Addresses a real and documented limitation: The observation that existing models oversample helix bundles (73% vs 42% PDB helicity) is well-documented, and the expanded Pareto frontiers between designability, diversity, and novelty represent meaningful practical improvements.

Thorough evaluation: Comprehensive sweep over 1,750 inference settings with proper ablations, introduction of six ellipsoid consistency metrics, and demonstration of out-of-distribution generalization (massive β-barrels, extreme helix bundles).
Intentionally simple statistical model for ellipsoid layouts is a strength—avoiding mode collapse while guaranteeing novelty and diversity of generated layouts.
weaknesses: No experimental (wet-lab) validation: All evaluation is purely computational using the ESMFold/ProteinMPNN pipeline (scRMSD < 2Å), which, while standard, is a known imperfect proxy for actual foldability and function.

Limited to secondary structure annotations (α/β): The ellipsoid semantics do not capture functional annotations, binding sites, or other biologically relevant properties, limiting the practical utility for targeted protein design.

Comparison fairness: The comparison with Chroma's ShapeConditioner may be somewhat unfair since Chroma was not specifically designed for this conditioning type, and no comparison is made with other controllable generation approaches like motif scaffolding methods.

Segmentation sensitivity: The fixed 5Å threshold in the segmentation algorithm is a potentially fragile design choice that may not generalize across different protein sizes and topologies without manual tuning.

Dependence on Multiflow: The approach is demonstrated only on top of Multiflow; generalizability to other base models (RFDiffusion, Chroma, FrameFlow) is not explored, making it unclear how much the contribution is architecture-specific.
The theoretical guarantees for classifier-free guidance break for SO(3) probability paths, and the paper relies on empirical success without providing deeper analysis of when or why this might fail.
review.justification: ProtComposer makes a meaningful and novel contribution by introducing spatial layout control to protein structure generation through an intuitive ellipsoid representation, filling a genuine capability gap. The technical execution is strong with principled SE(3)-equivariant design and comprehensive computational evaluation. While the lack of experimental validation and limitation to secondary structure annotations constrain the immediate practical impact, the demonstrated improvements in diversity, the new structural editing capabilities, and the expanded Pareto frontiers represent solid advances that meet the acceptance threshold for a poster presentation at ICLR.

detailed_results.title: BigDocs: An Open Dataset for Training Multimodal Models on Document and Code Tasks

review.summary: BigDocs introduces a 7.5M-sample, license-permissive multimodal dataset for document understanding and code generation, along with 10 novel benchmark tasks (BigDocs-Bench) focused on structured output generation from visual inputs, and demonstrates competitive performance across four model architectures.
strengths: Addresses a genuine and important bottleneck: ~80% of surveyed document datasets have restrictive licensing, and BigDocs fills this gap with rigorous per-sample license auditing and metadata tracking

BigDocs-Bench introduces genuinely novel and practically relevant evaluation tasks (Screenshot2HTML, Image2Flow, GUI reasoning, Image2LaTeX) with appropriate metrics (Tree Edit Distance, LST F1, DINOScore) that test capabilities largely absent from existing benchmarks
Thorough contamination analysis using CLIP similarity at multiple thresholds, showing generally lower contamination than DocStruct4M—this transparency is commendable
Comprehensive experimental setup across four diverse architectures (DocOwl1.5-8B, Qwen2-VL-2B, Phi-3.5-Vision-4B, LLaVA-NeXT-7B) with consistent training protocols
The unified metadata framework with per-sample provenance tracking sets a good standard for dataset documentation practices in the field
Human evaluation (88% preference over Phi3.5 Instruct, 63% over GPT-4o) provides meaningful subjective validation
weaknesses: The headline claim of '25.8% improvement over GPT-4o' on BigDocs-Bench compares finetuned models (trained on BigDocs-Bench training splits) against zero/few-shot baselines—this is not an apples-to-apples comparison and the framing in the abstract is misleading

Several BigDocs-Bench tasks rely on synthetic annotations from other models (GUI2Summary from InternVL2-8B, GUI-VQA from LLaMA 3.1-8b, Chart2Caption from InternVL2-26B), introducing quality ceilings and potential error propagation; the '99% pass rate' for manual verification lacks sufficient detail

On standard document understanding benchmarks (Table 2), improvements over DocStruct4M are marginal (e.g., 60.80% vs 60.51% for Phi3.5-Vision), suggesting the primary advantage is licensing compliance rather than data quality
The base of only 16 retained datasets after license filtering is narrow; the heavy reliance on newly crawled data whose quality guarantees are less established could be a concern
Limited analysis of failure modes and limitations of the benchmark tasks—when do models trained on BigDocs fail, and what types of documents or structured outputs remain challenging?
The paper would benefit from ablation studies on dataset composition—which of the 30 tasks contribute most to downstream performance, and what is the effect of dataset scale?
review.justification: BigDocs makes a solid resource contribution by addressing the real and important problem of license-permissive training data for document AI, introducing genuinely novel benchmark tasks for structured output generation, and establishing good practices for dataset documentation. While the technical novelty is limited, the misleading framing of the GPT-4o comparison and reliance on synthetic annotations are concerns, and standard benchmark gains are marginal, the practical value to the community—especially researchers constrained by licensing—and the novel benchmark tasks justify acceptance as a poster.

detailed_results.title: Learning Partial Graph Matching via Optimal Partial Transport

review.summary: This paper proposes a novel optimization framework for partial graph matching inspired by optimal partial transport, using weighted total variation as the divergence function. The key contribution is a theoretically justified reduction of partial graph matching to the Linear Sum Assignment Problem (LSAP), enabling exact polynomial-time solutions via the Hungarian algorithm, combined with an end-to-end deep learning architecture featuring learnable matching biases and a partial matching loss.
strengths: Clean and elegant theoretical framework: The reduction from partial graph matching to LSAP via optimal partial transport with weighted TV divergence is non-trivial and well-justified through formal proofs (Theorems 5.1, 5.2, 6.2, Lemma 6.1).
Guaranteed polynomial-time exact solutions (O(n³) worst case), unlike ILP-based approaches or methods requiring separate estimation of matching cardinality k.

The matching bias concept is well-motivated—it encodes node matchability directly from the affinity matrix without requiring class-specific prior knowledge, providing a principled mechanism to decide which nodes should remain unmatched.
Significant inference speedup (~3x over competitors per Table 2, right) with consistent improvements on high-quality benchmarks (SPair-71K, IMCPT 100).
The connection between optimal transport and combinatorial partial matching is timely and could inspire similar reductions for related problems (partial shape matching, network alignment).
Comprehensive experimental setup with ablation studies on matching bias formulations, hyperparameter sensitivity, and noise robustness.
weaknesses: Empirical improvements, while consistent, are relatively modest in absolute terms (~2 F1 points on SPair-71K), making it hard to assess practical significance beyond the theoretical elegance.

Sensitivity to hyperparameter ρ (Figure 2, middle) is concerning—performance varies meaningfully, and the paper provides no principled guidance for setting ρ in new domains, which limits practical applicability.

Performance on Pascal VOC is slightly worse than some baselines; the explanation attributing this to annotation quality deserves more rigorous scrutiny (e.g., annotation quality analysis or controlled experiments).

The matching bias formulation (max affinity per row/column through sigmoid) is ultimately heuristic despite being intuitively motivated; the ablation (Table 3) shows other designs perform comparably, suggesting this component is not strongly justified.

Limited evaluation on truly large-scale or highly asymmetric graphs, where the augmented cost matrix construction (adding n-m dummy nodes) could become expensive and the method's practical advantages over alternatives are untested.
The paper's conclusion appears truncated, and limitations are not thoroughly discussed—important for a method with clear trade-offs (ρ sensitivity, assumption of m ≤ n, etc.).
review.justification: The paper makes a genuine theoretical contribution by elegantly connecting partial graph matching to LSAP through optimal partial transport with weighted TV divergence, yielding polynomial-time exact solutions. The framework is well-formulated with complete proofs. While empirical improvements are modest and there are concerns about hyperparameter sensitivity and limited large-scale evaluation, the theoretical elegance, practical speedup, and consistent improvements on high-quality benchmarks collectively meet the acceptance bar. The work opens promising directions at the intersection of optimal transport and combinatorial matching.

detailed_results.title: CAKE: Cascading and Adaptive KV Cache Eviction with Layer Preferences

review.summary: CAKE proposes a training-free KV cache eviction framework that adaptively allocates cache budgets across transformer layers based on spatial-temporal attention dynamics (entropy for dispersion, variance for temporal shift), manages memory constraints through a provably equivalent cascading eviction scheme, and uses a variance-aware eviction indicator to handle shifting token importance over time.
strengths: Comprehensive experimental evaluation across 5 LLM architectures (7B-70B), two benchmarks (LongBench, NeedleBench), six budget levels, and five baselines, providing strong evidence of generalizability

Clean theoretical contributions: Proposition 1 proves monotonic budget decrease across cascading stages, and Theorem 1 establishes equivalence between cascading and single-pass eviction, ensuring no approximation error
Strong modularity — the allocation strategy demonstrably improves existing methods (H2O, SnapKV) as a plug-in replacement, increasing practical adoption potential
Particularly strong performance under tight memory constraints (64L-256L), which is precisely the regime most relevant for real deployment scenarios

End-to-end practical validation including actual memory savings (~48.63% peak memory reduction at 128K) and >10× decoding latency speedup with FlashAttention-2, going beyond theoretical cache reduction
weaknesses: Limited novelty in individual components: entropy-based dispersion, variance-based temporal shift, and mean+variance eviction indicators are straightforward applications of standard statistical measures; the contribution is primarily in their integration rather than in any fundamentally new insight

Hyperparameter sensitivity (τ₁, τ₂, γ, observation window Sw) is insufficiently analyzed in the main text — practical deployment requires understanding robustness to these choices across diverse models and tasks

Computational overhead of the cascading approach (repeated preference computation and multi-stage eviction during prefilling) is not thoroughly analyzed; the paper focuses on memory savings but underreports additional computation
The comparison baseline set, while reasonable, lacks some concurrent 2024 methods (e.g., Quest, MInference) that would strengthen the evaluation

Limited analysis of failure modes or scenarios where the spatial-temporal preference metrics might be misleading (e.g., for models with very different architectural choices or attention patterns that don't follow the observed entropy/variance patterns)
review.justification: CAKE addresses a practically important bottleneck in LLM inference with a well-engineered, theoretically grounded framework. The cascading equivalence theorem is a clean result, the experiments are comprehensive and demonstrate consistent improvements especially under tight memory budgets, and the modularity as a plug-in enhancement is valuable. However, the novelty of individual components is incremental (standard statistical measures applied to a known heterogeneity problem), and the hyperparameter sensitivity and computational overhead deserve more attention. The overall contribution is solid and meets the acceptance bar as a well-executed systems/efficiency paper.

detailed_results.title: Fine-tuning with Reserved Majority for Noise Reduction

review.summary: This paper proposes NoRM (Noise Reduction with Reserved Majority), a post-training method that decomposes LoRA fine-tuned parameters via randomized SVD and retains only major components whose subspace aligns with pretrained weights, determined by a Grassmann distance-based search (Sim-Search). The approach is framed within a broader PREFT (Parameter-Redundant Fine-Tuning) framework and shows consistent improvements over vanilla LoRA across instruction tuning, math reasoning, and code generation tasks.
strengths: Thorough pilot experiments (Section 2.2) systematically investigate redundancy at holistic, layer-wise, and module-wise granularities, providing compelling motivation for the approach. The finding that random dropping of LoRA channels improves performance even at r=16 is striking.

Comprehensive evaluation spanning three pretrained models (Qwen2-7B, Llama3-8B, Mistral-7B), three task domains, and five PEFT baselines, with consistent improvements demonstrated across all settings.

High practical utility: NoRM requires no modifications to the LoRA training pipeline, incurs zero additional inference latency, and can be applied as a simple post-processing step, making it immediately deployable.

Thorough ablation studies comparing five alternative component selection strategies (minimum similarity, minor components, L2 distance, cosine similarity, PCA), clearly validating the Sim-Search design choice.
The demonstration that NoRM enables monotonic improvement with increasing rank (Figure 4a) addresses a significant practical pain point in rank selection for LoRA users.
The forgetting analysis (Table 4) and amplification factor analysis (Table 5) provide useful mechanistic understanding of why the method works.
weaknesses: Limited novelty: The core technical contribution—applying SVD to delta weights and selecting components via a subspace similarity criterion—is relatively straightforward. The PREFT framework largely formalizes existing approaches (TAIA, MedCare) rather than introducing fundamentally new concepts.

No theoretical analysis: There are no formal guarantees on when or why subspace similarity correlates with reduced hallucination or improved downstream performance. The method remains an empirical heuristic without theoretical grounding.

Main experimental results appear to be single runs without confidence intervals or statistical significance tests, despite the pilot experiments using five seeds. This is concerning for claims of consistent improvement.
Using instruction-tuned models (e.g., Qwen2-7B-Instruct) as base models is a confounding factor that makes it harder to isolate NoRM's contribution from effects of prior alignment tuning.
The randomized SVD introduces stochasticity via the random matrix Ω, yet the paper doesn't characterize variance across different random seeds for this decomposition or discuss its stability.
Limited model scale: All experiments use 7-8B parameter models. Scaling behavior to larger models (70B+) is unknown, which limits the generalizability claims.

The Sim-Search procedure requires iterating over τ steps with SVD computations per parameter matrix. While a one-time cost, the computational overhead is not clearly quantified, especially for larger models.
review.justification: The paper addresses a timely and practical problem in LoRA fine-tuning with a clean, easy-to-deploy solution backed by comprehensive experiments across multiple models and tasks. The consistent empirical improvements and strong ablation studies demonstrate the method's effectiveness. However, the novelty is moderate—the core technique of SVD-based component selection with subspace similarity is relatively standard—and the lack of theoretical analysis or formal guarantees limits the intellectual contribution. The practical utility and thorough experimentation push it above the acceptance threshold as a solid poster contribution.

detailed_results.title: Learning Structured Representations by Embedding Class Hierarchy with Fast Optimal Transport

review.summary: The paper proposes replacing ℓ₂ distances between class centroids with Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) in the CPCC regularization framework for learning hierarchically structured representations. The authors develop a family of OT-CPCC variants, with the standout contribution being FastFT—a linear-time EMD approximation that exploits the label hierarchy tree structure—and show that the EMD formulation generalizes the prior ℓ₂-CPCC method (recovering it under Gaussian assumptions).
strengths: Clean theoretical framework: Proposition 3.1 elegantly shows EMD-CPCC generalizes ℓ₂-CPCC by recovering it under shared-covariance Gaussian assumptions, providing a principled justification for the approach.

The FastFT algorithm is the standout contribution—leveraging the given label hierarchy to avoid expensive tree construction yields O((m+n)d) linear-time complexity, which is both theoretically elegant and practically important for scalability.

The gradient analysis in Section 3.3 provides genuine mechanistic insight: EMD gradients assign heterogeneous importance to data pairs via the optimal transport plan, while ℓ₂ gradients weight all samples uniformly.

Comprehensive experimental evaluation spanning 7 datasets (including large-scale iNaturalist), multiple evaluation protocols (classification, retrieval, interpretability), and thorough ablations over batch size, regularization strength, and architecture.
The GMM analysis (Figure 5) empirically validates the multi-modality assumption that motivates the entire approach, strengthening the paper's narrative.
Code availability enhances reproducibility.
weaknesses: Accuracy improvements over ℓ₂-CPCC are often modest (typically ~1%), with the most convincing gains appearing in TestCPCC and retrieval metrics rather than the primary classification task. This raises questions about practical significance.
No single OT-CPCC variant dominates across all settings—FastFT excels on interpretability, SWD sometimes wins on fine-grained tasks—making practical guidance for practitioners unclear.
The method requires a predefined label hierarchy, limiting applicability to domains where such hierarchies are available or can be meaningfully constructed.

On BREEDS datasets where features are less multi-modal (as acknowledged by the authors), OT-CPCC sometimes slightly underperforms ℓ₂-CPCC, suggesting the benefits are situation-dependent and the method does not uniformly improve upon the baseline.
The paper does not investigate how the quality or granularity of the provided hierarchy affects results in the main text, which is important for understanding robustness.
The core idea of replacing centroid distances with OT distances is relatively straightforward; the novelty primarily lies in the FastFT algorithm rather than the conceptual framework.
review.justification: The paper makes a clean theoretical contribution by generalizing CPCC with optimal transport distances and introduces the elegant FastFT algorithm with provably linear-time complexity. The experimental evaluation is thorough and the theoretical analysis is rigorous. However, empirical improvements over the baseline are often modest and situation-dependent, and the conceptual novelty beyond FastFT is limited. Overall, the combination of solid theory, a useful algorithmic contribution, and comprehensive (if incrementally improving) experiments meets the acceptance bar for a poster.

detailed_results.title: How efficient is LLM-generated code? A rigorous & high-standard benchmark

review.summary: The paper introduces ENAMEL, a benchmark for evaluating the efficiency (not just correctness) of LLM-generated code, featuring a new metric eff@k with a Rao-Blackwellized unbiased variance-reduced estimator, expert-written reference solutions for 142 HumanEval problems, and expert-curated test case generators. An evaluation of 30 LLMs reveals a substantial gap between correctness and efficiency (e.g., GPT-4: pass@1=0.831 vs eff@1=0.454).
strengths: Addresses a genuine and timely gap: systematic efficiency evaluation of LLM-generated code with rigorous methodology, going beyond the saturated correctness-only paradigm
