The ablation study systematically validates each component (key neuron locating, ability weight extraction, tensor selection, transfer mechanism), demonstrating meaningful individual contributions.
The interpretability analysis showing MLP layers carry more ability-related knowledge than attention layers provides useful insights for the community.
weaknesses: The 'training-free transfer' framing is misleading: the pipeline still requires continual pre-training on ability-related and language-related neurons separately to produce component models. Only the final merging stage is training-free.

Empirical improvements are modest and inconsistent: average math accuracy improves from 34.10 to 37.20 (9.1% relative), science from 42.05 to 43.06 (2.4% relative), and MAET actually underperforms F-CPT L&A on Telugu math (25.20 vs. 27.60), undermining the low-resource narrative.
The linear decomposability assumption (that ability and language knowledge occupy separable subspaces in parameter space) is strong and lacks theoretical justification beyond empirical results.

Heavy hyperparameter burden (α, β, γ, η, μ_i, k1%, k2%) with limited sensitivity analysis — optimal k2% differs substantially between tasks (80% for math, 60% for science), raising generalizability concerns.

Limited evaluation scope: one primary backbone (LLaMA-3 8B), only two ability domains, four target languages (two high-resource), and no statistical significance tests despite small evaluation sets (MGSM: 250 examples per language).
The gradient approximation in Equation 1 (using weight change magnitude as proxy for accumulated gradients) is coarse and entirely unvalidated — no analysis shows this approximation is reasonable.

The low-resource framing is weakened by the assumption that general multilingual corpora are available for all target languages and that the method still requires language-specific continual pre-training data.
review.justification: While MAET presents an interesting conceptual framework for cross-lingual ability transfer through parameter decomposition, the paper falls short in several critical areas. The empirical improvements are modest and inconsistent across languages (sometimes underperforming baselines for the very low-resource scenarios it targets), the core assumption of linear decomposability lacks theoretical grounding, the evaluation scope is narrow (one backbone, two tasks, four languages), and the numerous unvalidated approximations and heavy hyperparameter dependence limit practical applicability. The strongest result — OOD preservation — is valuable but insufficient to compensate for these weaknesses.

detailed_results.title: NEXTLOCLLM: NEXT LOCATION PREDICTION USING LLMS

review.summary: NextLocLLM proposes integrating LLMs into next location prediction by encoding locations via normalized continuous spatial coordinates, creating LLM-enhanced POI embeddings from natural language descriptions, using a partially frozen LLM backbone, and employing a KD-tree-based prediction retrieval module. The approach aims to improve both supervised performance and cross-city zero-shot generalization.
strengths: Clean problem formulation that identifies concrete and real limitations of prior prompt-based LLM approaches for next location prediction (structured output failures, inability to encode spatial relationships, etc.)

The coordinate normalization strategy using Web Mercator projection with standard normal scaling is intuitive, simple, and effective for enabling cross-city transfer — this is a broadly applicable insight
Comprehensive experimental setup spanning four diverse geographic datasets (China, Japan, US) with multiple evaluation metrics
Modular framework design where each component's contribution is individually evaluated through ablation studies
Honest reporting of prompt-based LLM failures (structural output issues in Appendix B) adds transparency
weaknesses: The finding that GPT-2 (124M params) performs comparably to Llama2-7B and Llama3-8B (Appendix N) fundamentally undermines the claim that LLM-specific capabilities are being leveraged — this suggests the backbone acts merely as a generic transformer encoder, not as an LLM

The zero-shot comparison is unfair: NextLocLLM is trained on other city datasets (transfer learning) while LLMMob and ZS-NL use prompt-based approaches without any training data, making the comparison asymmetric and the 'zero-shot' framing misleading

No statistical analysis — no confidence intervals, significance tests, or variance across runs are reported, yet some results show NextLocLLM underperforming baselines (e.g., C-MHSA on Japan Hit@1), making claimed superiority unconvincing
Ablation studies conducted on only one dataset (Xi'an), limiting the generalizability of architectural insights across diverse settings
Cross-city transfer results are highly variable (3.85% to 37.02% Hit@1), suggesting fragility of the approach without adequate analysis of when/why it fails
No computational cost analysis relative to baselines despite using LLM backbones — this is critical for practical applicability
The regression-based loss function (Euclidean distance on coordinates) is fundamentally different from classification-based baselines, making direct performance comparisons less straightforward
review.justification: While the paper addresses a genuine problem and proposes some useful ideas (coordinate normalization for cross-city transfer), its core claim of leveraging LLM capabilities is undermined by GPT-2 matching much larger models' performance. The experimental evaluation has significant fairness issues in zero-shot comparisons, lacks statistical rigor, and shows inconsistent improvements. The novelty is limited as the approach primarily adapts existing techniques (One Fits All's partially frozen transformer, standard KD-tree retrieval) to the spatial domain without sufficient evidence that LLM-specific pretraining provides meaningful benefit over generic transformer encoding.

detailed_results.title: Disconnecting The Dots: Creating Leakage-Free Protein Datasets by Removal of Densely Connected Data Points

review.summary: The paper proposes a data splitting methodology for protein datasets that removes highly connected 'hub' nodes from similarity graphs to create leakage-free train/test splits, enabling evaluation of model generalization under stringent similarity thresholds where traditional clustering methods fail to produce well-separated partitions.
strengths: Addresses a genuinely important and well-motivated problem: data leakage in biological datasets due to evolutionary relationships, with convincing quantification showing ~80% of test proteins in existing benchmarks exceed stated similarity thresholds
The multi-threshold evaluation framework is elegant and scientifically informative, enabling assessment across a continuum of generalization difficulty in a single pipeline run
Practical and efficient implementation: the method achieves minimal data loss (1.4% for sequence-based, ~12% for structure-based splitting) while processing ~30K proteins in minutes

Thorough experimental design spanning four prediction tasks (EC, GO-BP/CC/MF), five protein representations, both sequence and structure similarity metrics, and 10 random splits with aggregated statistics

Valuable biological insights, including that PLMs outperform structure-based representations even on structure-based splits, and that 30% sequence identity is approximately as challenging as 90% TM-score structural similarity
weaknesses: Critical absence of direct head-to-head quantitative benchmarks against competing methods (GraphPart, Lo-Hi, CCPart) on identical datasets with identical metrics—the comparison remains largely qualitative, which significantly undermines claims of superiority
No formal theoretical analysis: no bounds on data loss, no convergence guarantees, no approximation ratio relative to optimal solutions for the hub removal heuristic

Sensitivity analysis of the Leiden resolution parameter r is insufficient—this introduces a tunable hyperparameter whose effect on downstream split quality and model evaluation deserves deeper investigation
Scalability to larger datasets (e.g., UniProt-scale ~250M sequences) is untested, and the pairwise similarity computation may become prohibitive at such scales
Limited methodological novelty: the approach combines existing components (Leiden community detection + greedy hub removal) in a straightforward manner without deep algorithmic innovation
Demonstration limited to protein function prediction only—claimed data-agnostic generality is unsubstantiated without validation on other biological domains (small molecules, nucleic acids, etc.)
review.justification: While the paper addresses a genuinely important problem and provides valuable quantification of existing dataset leakage, the core methodological contribution—community detection plus greedy hub removal—is relatively straightforward with limited algorithmic novelty. The most critical weakness is the absence of direct quantitative comparisons with competing methods (GraphPart, Lo-Hi, CCPart), which are acknowledged but not benchmarked against, making it impossible to assess whether this approach actually improves upon existing solutions. The lack of theoretical guarantees and limited scalability analysis further weaken the contribution for a venue like ICLR.

detailed_results.title: Saturn: Sample-efficient Generative Molecular Design using Memory Manipulation

review.summary: Saturn introduces the first application of the Mamba state-space model architecture for goal-directed molecular generation, combined with the Augmented Memory algorithm. The paper provides mechanistic insight into how experience replay with SMILES augmentation enables a 'hop-and-locally-explore' behavior in chemical space, achieving strong sample efficiency and outperforming 22 baselines on drug-discovery-relevant multi-parameter optimization tasks.
strengths: Exceptional experimental rigor: over 5,000 experiments across 10 seeds each, with a well-structured three-part evaluation (toy MPO, docking MPO, benchmarking) providing logical progression of evidence.

The mechanistic analysis is the paper's standout contribution—decomposing how SMILES augmentation interacts with likelihood dynamics, demonstrating the near-Dirac token distributions, and revealing the 'hop-and-locally-explore' phenomenon through UMAP trajectories and intra/inter-chunk Tanimoto similarity is genuinely insightful.

Strong practical motivation tied to real drug discovery workflows: the argument that improved sample efficiency could enable direct optimization of expensive high-fidelity oracles addresses a genuine bottleneck in the field.
Honest reporting of limitations, including poor Novel Hit Ratios without explicit Tanimoto constraints and high variance across seeds, which builds trust in the results.
Timely introduction of Mamba to molecular generation, with its linear scaling potentially advantageous for longer molecular representations.
weaknesses: The paper appears truncated mid-sentence in Section 4.3, preventing full evaluation of the benchmarking discussion—this is a serious submission quality issue.

High variance across seeds (e.g., 57.981±18.537 Hit Ratio for PARP1) suggests brittleness that undermines practical deployment claims; the paper doesn't adequately address strategies to mitigate this.

Limited fundamental novelty: all individual algorithmic components (Augmented Memory, experience replay, SMILES augmentation, diversity filters) existed previously; the contribution is primarily their combination with Mamba, which is more engineering integration than methodological innovation.

The diversity-efficiency trade-off is insufficiently addressed: poor Novel Hit Ratios without explicit Tanimoto constraints reveal the local sampling strategy primarily generates incremental modifications to known molecules, which may limit practical utility for discovering genuinely novel scaffolds.

Benchmarking partially relies on results reported by other papers rather than independent reproduction, and the comparison with GEAM underestimates its hidden oracle cost (pre-computed docking for entire ZINC 250k). No experimental or MD validation is provided despite the framing around enabling high-fidelity oracle optimization.
review.justification: Saturn makes a solid contribution through its extensive experimental validation and genuinely insightful mechanistic analysis of why experience replay with SMILES augmentation works, providing the 'hop-and-locally-explore' explanation. The introduction of Mamba to molecular generation is timely and practically motivated. However, the limited fundamental novelty (combination of existing components), high variance across seeds, diversity limitations, apparent truncation, and lack of experimental validation temper the impact. The paper narrowly meets the acceptance bar primarily on the strength of its mechanistic insights and experimental thoroughness, though the concerns are substantial enough to limit confidence in this assessment.

detailed_results.title: Enhancing Cross-Lingual and Cross-Domain Adaptability in Large Language Models for Software Engineering

review.summary: The paper proposes UDA-EDCM, a mathematical framework for unsupervised domain adaptation in cross-lingual and cross-domain code modeling, combining measure theory, optimal transport, differential geometry, and information theory. The framework is evaluated on code generation and summarization tasks across multiple programming languages.
strengths: The problem of cross-lingual and cross-domain adaptation for code intelligence is timely and practically relevant.
The breadth of mathematical tools assembled (optimal transport, Riemannian geometry, information geometry, functional analysis) is ambitious in scope.
The experimental evaluation covers multiple programming languages and coding tasks including zero-shot and few-shot scenarios.
weaknesses: Fundamental disconnect between theory and practice: seven theorems are presented but no concrete algorithm is derived from them, and the experimental system's relationship to the theoretical framework is never explained.

Most theorems are restatements of well-known results (Kantorovich duality, Ben-David domain adaptation bounds, spectral theorem for compact operators, Gong et al.'s Geodesic Flow Kernel) without adequate attribution or genuine novelty.
Missing implementation details: no model architecture, training procedure, hyperparameter specification, or algorithmic description is provided, making reproducibility impossible.
Naming inconsistency between UDA-EDCM and AdaptiCode-ML raises serious concerns about manuscript integrity and preparation quality.
No ablation studies isolating the contribution of each theoretical component, which is essential for a multi-component framework.

Weak baselines limited to mT5, PLBART, and ERNIE-Code with no comparison to recent code LLMs (CodeLlama, StarCoder); statistical claims (p < 0.01, Cohen's d) are asserted without presenting actual statistics.
Inappropriate hyperbolic language ('groundbreaking,' 'revolutionary') for a scientific manuscript; the 'unified theorem' mentioned in the abstract is apparently never stated in the paper.
review.justification: The paper suffers from a fundamental theory-practice disconnect: an extensive mathematical framework is presented but never connected to an implementable algorithm or the evaluated system. The theorems are largely well-known results repackaged without proper attribution, the experimental evaluation lacks modern baselines and ablation studies, and critical implementation details are entirely absent, making the work neither reproducible nor verifiable. The naming inconsistencies and hyperbolic language further undermine confidence in the manuscript's integrity.

detailed_results.title: Small Molecule Optimization with Large Language Models

review.summary: This paper presents a molecular optimization framework combining domain-specific LLMs (trained on 110M+ molecules with structured property annotations) with an evolutionary optimization algorithm that uses prompt-based generation, rejection sampling, and dynamic fine-tuning. The approach achieves new SOTA on the PMO benchmark (~8% improvement) and competitive/superior results on docking-based multi-property optimization tasks.
strengths: Comprehensive end-to-end framework spanning corpus construction, model pretraining, property prediction evaluation, and optimization, providing a cohesive contribution rather than isolated improvements

Strong empirical results: ~8% improvement on PMO benchmark (17.534 vs 16.213), 4x sample efficiency gains on docking tasks, and competitive MoleculeNet results, all evaluated with standard protocols (5 seeds)
Thoughtful training corpus design with structured tagged key-value property annotations, randomized property ordering, and SMILES placement to prevent positional biases
Novel dual use of the LLM as both molecular generator (via similarity-conditioned prompts acting as crossover/mutation operators) and oracle modeler (via online fine-tuning on high-scoring molecules)
Rigorous novelty analysis demonstrating generated molecules are genuinely novel (Tanimoto similarity < 0.3 to PubChem), addressing memorization concerns
Full open-source release of training data, models, and optimization code, significantly enhancing reproducibility and community impact
weaknesses: No clean ablation study disentangling contributions of the evolutionary prompt strategy, rejection sampling, and online fine-tuning components—it's unclear which elements are essential vs. marginal
Inconsistent performance across docking targets (e.g., persistent 5ht1b underperformance vs. Saturn) without deep analysis of why certain targets are more challenging
Sensitivity to numerical precision (bfloat16 vs. full precision) is concerning for reproducibility and practical deployment
Fragmented comparison landscape: different baselines appear in different experiments (PMO vs. docking benchmarks), making holistic assessment of the method difficult
Computational cost of dynamic fine-tuning during optimization is not thoroughly discussed; scalability to larger models or more expensive oracles is unclear
No treatment of synthesizability constraints or retrosynthetic feasibility, which are critical for practical drug design applications
Hyperparameter tuning strategies differ across benchmarks, raising questions about the algorithm's robustness without task-specific tuning
review.justification: The paper makes a solid empirical contribution with new SOTA on the established PMO benchmark and competitive docking results, backed by a well-designed training corpus and thoughtful optimization algorithm. The open-source release adds substantial value. However, the absence of clean ablation studies to isolate the contribution of individual algorithmic components, inconsistent performance across targets, and sensitivity to numerical precision prevent stronger confidence. The work advances the field meaningfully but leaves important analytical questions unanswered.

detailed_results.title: OptiBench: Benchmarking Large Language Models in Optimization Modeling with Equivalence-Detection Evaluation

review.summary: OptiBench introduces a benchmark of 816 LP/MILP optimization word problems spanning 16 problem classes and 80+ domains, paired with a novel evaluation method based on a modified Weisfeiler-Lehman graph isomorphism test that determines optimization model equivalence with theoretical guarantees. Benchmarking reveals GPT-4o achieves 49.39% overall accuracy with dramatic variation across problem classes, outperforming reasoning-augmented models (o1) and agent-based approaches.
strengths: Novel evaluation methodology: The WL-test-based equivalence detection for optimization models is a genuinely creative approach with formal theoretical guarantees (Theorem 4.1), moving beyond unreliable solver-based evaluation that fails on NP-hard MILP instances.
Model-data separation format mirrors real-world OR practice, enabling evaluation of modeling ability independent of data handling—a critical design choice for practical relevance.

Comprehensive and well-structured dataset with hierarchical reverse data evolution pipeline ensuring ground truth validity, covering diverse problem types, classes, and domains with two abstraction levels.

Revealing empirical findings: the underperformance of reasoning-focused models (o1) and agents versus direct GPT-4o prompting, and dramatic class-level variation (>90% knapsack, <5% TSP), provide actionable insights for the community.
Timely contribution addressing a genuine gap—the community urgently needs standardized benchmarks and evaluation tools as LLM-for-OR applications rapidly grow.
weaknesses: Critical missing analysis: No false negative study quantifying how often the equivalence detection rejects correct but differently-formulated models (e.g., different valid linearizations, alternative big-M formulations, auxiliary variable introductions). This undermines confidence in all reported accuracy numbers.

Circularity concern: Dataset generated primarily by GPT-4o raises validity questions when benchmarking GPT-4o itself—the model may have systematic biases aligned with its own generation patterns, potentially inflating its performance relative to other LLMs.

Insufficient statistical rigor: No confidence intervals, significance tests, or multiple runs reported to account for LLM stochasticity. With only ~50 problems per class, per-class accuracy figures may have high variance.

The paper claims 'almost all instances' satisfy the sufficient conditions for WL-test correctness but doesn't provide exact statistics or analysis of what happens for instances that don't satisfy these conditions.
No comparison between the proposed evaluation method and solver-based evaluation on solvable instances to validate concordance and quantify the false negative rate of the structural approach.
Scope limited to LP/MILP, excluding nonlinear, stochastic, and multi-objective optimization—while acknowledged, this significantly limits the benchmark's coverage of real-world OR practice.
review.justification: The paper addresses a genuine need with a novel evaluation methodology grounded in formal theory, and the benchmark fills an important gap. However, the absence of false negative analysis for the evaluation method, the GPT-4o circularity in dataset construction, and lack of statistical rigor in experiments are significant concerns that prevent a stronger recommendation. The contribution is sufficient for a poster given its timeliness and the creative evaluation approach, but the experimental validation needs strengthening to fully support the claims.

detailed_results.title: Second-Order Algorithms for Finding Local Nash Equilibria in Zero-Sum Games

review.summary: This paper develops second-order algorithms (DND, SecOND, SeCoND) for finding local Nash equilibria in smooth, possibly nonconvex-nonconcave zero-sum games, providing the first local convergence rate guarantees (linear and superlinear) for methods that provably converge to only Nash equilibria, with extensions to constrained settings.
strengths: First convergence rate guarantees (linear and superlinear) for algorithms that converge exclusively to local Nash equilibria in nonconvex-nonconcave zero-sum games, filling a genuine theoretical gap over prior work (LSS, CESP).

Elegant dynamical systems framework: the construction of the modified Jacobian dynamics that reduces to block-diagonal form at fixed points, effectively filtering out cross-player interaction terms responsible for non-Nash attractors, is both principled and insightful.

The Gauss-Newton reinterpretation (SecOND) provides a useful conceptual bridge between game-theoretic optimization and classical nonlinear programming, potentially inspiring future algorithmic development.
Natural and non-trivial extension to convex constrained settings with potentially coupled constraints (SeCoND), retaining convergence-to-only-Nash guarantees via variational inequality reformulation.
Corollary 1's guarantee of real eigenvalues at Nash equilibria directly addresses the well-documented rotational instability problem that plagues gradient-based methods in games.
weaknesses: Experiments are very limited in scale and scope: only a 2D toy example and a 1D mixture-of-Gaussians GAN. No large-scale experiments demonstrating practical applicability, and SecOND does not consistently outperform DND even in these simple settings.

No comparison against several relevant modern baselines (extragradient methods, optimistic gradient methods, Bregman proximal methods in nonconvex-nonconcave settings), making it difficult to assess practical competitiveness.

Strong assumptions that may be hard to verify: Assumption 2 (invertibility of J, ∇²ₓₓf, ∇²yyf at all critical points) and Assumption 3 (ω(z) ∉ null(J(z)ᵀ)) are non-trivial, and the paper provides limited guidance on when they hold in practice.

Only local convergence analysis is provided; no global convergence guarantees exist, meaning the method's behavior from arbitrary initialization is unknown and may require careful restarts or initialization strategies.
Computational cost of O((n+m)³) per iteration for matrix inversions severely limits scalability to high-dimensional problems typical in adversarial learning (e.g., GANs with neural networks).

The superlinear convergence of SecOND is only to a neighborhood of a critical point (not necessarily Nash), after which it switches to DND—the practical benefit depends on hyperparameter ε and problem structure in ways that are not well-characterized.
review.justification: The paper makes a genuine and meaningful theoretical contribution by providing the first convergence rate analysis for algorithms guaranteed to converge to only local Nash equilibria in nonconvex-nonconcave zero-sum games, with an elegant dynamical systems framework and a non-trivial constrained extension. These results fill a clear gap in the literature. However, the practical impact is significantly limited by strong assumptions, only local convergence guarantees, O((n+m)³) computational cost, and notably weak experimental validation that fails to demonstrate scalability or compare against modern baselines. The theoretical merit is sufficient for acceptance, but the paper would benefit substantially from more comprehensive experiments.

detailed_results.title: Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge

review.summary: This paper introduces a quantitative metric for measuring self-preference bias in LLM-as-a-judge, grounded in the Equal Opportunity fairness framework, demonstrates that GPT-4 exhibits significant self-preference bias, and proposes that the underlying mechanism is perplexity-driven—LLMs favor lower-perplexity text regardless of its origin, and their own outputs naturally have lower perplexity.
strengths: The formalization of self-preference bias through the Equal Opportunity fairness framework is clean, well-motivated, and provides a reusable diagnostic tool for the community.

The perplexity-based mechanistic explanation is a genuinely useful reframing—decomposing self-preference into familiarity preference rather than self-recognition—with actionable implications for mitigation.
Large-scale experiments using the Chatbot Arena dataset (33K dialogues) with real user queries and diverse model outputs provide ecological validity and substantial scale.
The paper is highly timely given the explosive adoption of LLM-as-a-judge in benchmarking, RLHF, and deployment decisions, and the findings have clear practical relevance.
The systematic progression from metric definition to bias measurement to mechanistic investigation is well-structured and easy to follow.
weaknesses: The causal claim linking perplexity to self-preference bias remains correlational. No interventional experiments (e.g., artificially modifying perplexity while holding semantic content constant) are conducted to establish that perplexity is the mechanism rather than a correlated surface feature like stylistic similarity or fluency.

The inability to compute perplexity for GPT-4 and GPT-3.5-Turbo—the models exhibiting the strongest self-preference bias—is a critical gap that undermines the paper's central thesis. The Llama-based proxy experiments only partially compensate.

Perplexity correlates with many legitimate quality indicators (fluency, coherence, grammaticality). The paper does not adequately disentangle 'legitimate' perplexity effects from 'biased' ones, making it unclear how much of the observed pattern reflects genuine bias versus reasonable quality assessment.

No mitigation strategies are implemented or evaluated, despite the paper's discussion suggesting several (e.g., perplexity-aware score adjustment, ensemble evaluation). This limits practical contribution.
Temperature sensitivity, the impact of prompt design on bias magnitude, and generalization to evaluation formats beyond pairwise comparison are not explored, limiting the robustness of the findings.
review.justification: While the paper addresses a timely and important problem with a well-formalized metric and an interesting perplexity-based hypothesis, the core causal claim remains correlational, the most critical model (GPT-4) cannot be analyzed within the perplexity framework, and the failure to disentangle legitimate quality signals from bias undermines the central thesis. The absence of interventional experiments and implemented mitigations leaves the contribution incomplete for the ICLR acceptance bar.

detailed_results.title: Understanding Layer Significance in LLM Alignment

review.summary: The paper proposes ILA, a method to identify important layers for LLM alignment by learning binary masks over LoRA incremental weight matrices. The key finding is that important layers are remarkably consistent (~90% Jaccard similarity) across different alignment datasets, and this insight can be exploited to improve efficiency by selectively tuning only critical layers.
strengths: The ~90% Jaccard similarity of important layers across substantially different alignment datasets (Alpaca-GPT4, LIMA, No Robots) is a striking empirical finding that provides genuine granular evidence for the 'alignment is superficial' hypothesis.

Comprehensive experimental coverage across multiple models (LLaMA 2-7B/13B, LLaMA 3.1-8B, Mistral-7B), three alignment datasets of varying sizes, and multiple evaluation dimensions (language understanding and conversational ability).

Practical implications are clear and actionable: freezing ~25% unimportant layers improves performance, and tuning only 10-30% of layers achieves competitive results with reduced cost (~33GB to ~18GB GPU memory with QLoRA).
Well-designed ablation studies including random layer selection, position-based selection, and cross-dataset evaluation effectively demonstrate that the identified layers are non-trivially important.
Modest computational overhead of the mask learning stage (~11 minutes) makes the approach practically feasible.
weaknesses: No statistical rigor: no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported despite often small performance differences (e.g., 0.1-0.3 points on MT-Bench), making it unclear whether claimed improvements are robust.

Lack of deeper mechanistic analysis of WHY certain layers are important—the paper identifies important layers but does not investigate their computational role (attention patterns, factual recall, formatting), significantly limiting the interpretability contribution promised by the title.

Limited baseline comparisons for layer selection: only AdaLoRA is compared. Layer-wise learning rate schedules, progressive layer freezing, structured pruning methods, and other layer importance metrics (e.g., gradient-based, Fisher information) would provide much stronger baselines.

The theoretical analysis (Theorem 2.1) is somewhat circular—it essentially states that if training reaches ε-stability, masks converge, which is largely expected and provides limited additional insight.

Exclusive focus on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) without addressing preference optimization methods (RLHF, DPO) that are increasingly dominant in practice, and no evaluation on safety alignment tasks, limiting the generalizability of claims about 'LLM alignment' broadly.
The binary mask granularity (entire linear layers) is relatively coarse; finer-grained analysis at the attention head or neuron level could yield more mechanistically informative results.
review.justification: While the cross-dataset consistency finding (~90% overlap of important layers) is genuinely interesting, the paper has significant shortcomings that place it below the acceptance bar: (1) lack of statistical rigor with no error bars or significance tests despite modest performance differences, (2) absence of mechanistic analysis explaining why certain layers matter, limiting the paper's interpretability contribution, and (3) insufficient baselines and scope (no RLHF/DPO, no safety tasks) despite broad claims about 'LLM alignment.' The methodology, while clean, is relatively incremental (binary mask learning over LoRA) and the theoretical contribution is limited.

detailed_results.title: Temporal Test-Time Adaptation with State-Space Models

review.summary: This paper formalizes Temporal Test-Time Adaptation (TempTTA) as a distinct problem where distribution shifts evolve gradually over time, and proposes STAD, a probabilistic state-space model that tracks evolving class prototypes using Kalman filtering (Gaussian) or variational EM (von Mises-Fisher) to dynamically adapt the classification head without labels or source data.
strengths: The formalization of TempTTA as a distinct setting, clearly distinguished from related problems (TDG, GDA, CTTA) via a useful comparison table, fills an important gap in the TTA literature that has over-relied on synthetic corruption benchmarks.
The probabilistic SSM framework is principled and elegant, particularly the vMF-softmax equivalence (Eq. 9) that naturally bridges the generative model with standard neural network classifiers.
Strong robustness to small batch sizes (down to batch size 1) and label shift—conditions that cause many existing TTA methods to collapse—makes the method highly relevant for real-world deployment.
STAD is the only method that consistently ranks best or second-best across all datasets and experimental conditions, demonstrating reliable performance where competitors show high variance.
The ablation study clearly demonstrates that explicitly modeling temporal dynamics (vs. static clustering as in T3A) is the key differentiator, validating the core design choice.
Lightweight last-layer-only adaptation makes the method compatible with foundation models and API-served embeddings without requiring backpropagation.
weaknesses: Absolute accuracy gains under pure covariate shift (without label shift) are modest—on EVIS, STAD-vMF essentially matches the source model (56.67 vs. 56.59), raising questions about when dynamics modeling truly helps beyond robustness to label shift.
Limited dataset scale and diversity: only three temporal shift datasets, all in computer vision. No experiments on NLP, tabular, or other modalities limit generalizability claims.

Hyperparameter sensitivity (κ_trans, κ_ems, window size, EM iterations) is not systematically characterized. Given that the method introduces several new hyperparameters requiring tuning without labeled validation data, this is a significant practical concern.
The independence assumption between class prototype dynamics ignores potential correlations (e.g., classes that shift together), which could be important for modeling structured temporal shifts.
No comparison with temporal domain generalization methods adapted to test-time settings, or with recent online learning approaches that also handle temporal non-stationarity.
The assumption that a frozen feature extractor produces well-clustered representations under shift is strong and may not hold for severe distribution shifts, fundamentally limiting the approach.
review.justification: The paper addresses a genuinely underexplored and practically important problem (temporal distribution shifts at test time) with a principled probabilistic framework. The consistent robustness advantages under challenging deployment conditions (small batches, label shift) are valuable, and the method is well-grounded mathematically. However, the modest absolute gains under pure covariate shift, limited empirical scope (vision-only, three datasets), and underexplored hyperparameter sensitivity prevent a stronger recommendation. The contribution is solid but incremental, meeting the acceptance bar primarily through problem identification and principled methodology rather than transformative empirical results.

detailed_results.title: ConDiff: A Challenging Dataset for Neural Solvers of Partial Differential Equations

review.summary: ConDiff introduces a benchmark dataset for scientific machine learning focused on the steady-state diffusion equation with discontinuous, high-contrast coefficients, sampled from Gaussian Random Fields across multiple covariance models and variance levels. The paper provides 28,800 samples across 24 PDE configurations and baselines four neural architectures, demonstrating significant performance degradation at high contrast levels.
strengths: Addresses a genuine gap in existing SciML benchmarks: high-contrast, discontinuous coefficients are practically important but underrepresented in datasets like PDEBench
The proposed contrast metric (Equation 6) provides a clear, interpretable measure of problem difficulty with demonstrated correlation to the condition number of the discretized operator
Open-source generation pipeline and Hugging Face hosting lower barriers to adoption and enable community extensions
Clear demonstration that existing neural PDE solvers degrade substantially with increasing contrast, motivating development of more robust architectures
Connection to real-world applications (e.g., SPE10 permeability field comparison) provides useful context for the problem's practical relevance
weaknesses: Extremely narrow scope: only one PDE type (2D steady-state linear elliptic), one domain (unit square), one boundary condition type (Dirichlet), limiting generalizability as a benchmark
Baseline experiments are shallow—only 4 models tested with no physics-informed approaches, no DeepONet, no message-passing architectures, and no analysis of why models fail at high contrast
No convergence study or verification of ground truth solutions: for high-contrast problems, numerical solver accuracy is non-trivial and should be validated
128×128 experiments exclude DilResNet and U-Net, making the benchmark incomplete at higher resolution
Transfer learning experiments (Tables 5-8) lack theoretical or mechanistic explanation for observed patterns
Maximum contrast (~10^5) is acknowledged to be an order of magnitude below realistic problems (SPE10 ~10^6), weakening the 'real-world relevance' argument
Dataset size (1000 train / 200 test per configuration) is relatively small and may not support data-hungry modern architectures
Training protocol (400-500 epochs with fixed schedule) appears somewhat arbitrary with no evidence of convergence
review.justification: While ConDiff addresses a real gap in SciML benchmarks—high-contrast discontinuous coefficients—the contribution is too narrow and incremental for a top venue. The dataset covers only a single 2D linear elliptic PDE on a regular grid, the baseline analysis is shallow with no investigation into failure mechanisms or physics-informed approaches, and the ground truth accuracy for high-contrast cases is unverified. The paper reads primarily as a dataset release without generating sufficient scientific insight or demonstrating broad enough utility to meet the ICLR acceptance bar.

detailed_results.title: MV-CLAM: Multi-View Molecular Interpretation with Cross-Modal Projection via Language Model

review.summary: MV-CLAM introduces MQ-Former, a multi-querying transformer that aligns 2D and 3D molecular representations into a unified text embedding space via shared self-attention layers, enabling LLM-based molecule captioning, retrieval, question answering, and zero-shot molecule editing.
strengths: Clean and elegant architectural design: shared self-attention across modality-specific transformer blocks is a principled and computationally efficient approach to multi-view molecular alignment, avoiding separate embedding spaces and redundant computation.

Consistent improvements across multiple tasks (retrieval, captioning, molecular QA) over recent strong baselines including 3D-MoLM and UniMoT, with particularly notable gains in molecular property prediction (e.g., molecular weight MAE: 21.35 vs. 42.76).

Well-motivated problem formulation: the paper clearly identifies three concrete issues with naive multi-modal alignment (separate embeddings, inconsistent text representations, computational overhead) and addresses each.

The claimed >50% training time reduction compared to processing views separately is practically significant, and the attention map visualizations provide intuitive evidence that 2D and 3D queries capture complementary molecular information.
The framework is generalizable beyond molecules—the MQ-Former architecture could be applied to other multi-modal scientific domains.
weaknesses: Single dataset evaluation (PubChem324k only) severely limits the generalizability of all claims. No cross-dataset evaluation or standard molecular benchmarks (e.g., MoleculeNet) are included.

Critical ablations are missing: (a) naive concatenation baseline where separate Q-Formers process each modality independently then concatenate, (b) ablation isolating the contribution of shared self-attention vs. simply having 2K query tokens instead of K, (c) late-fusion baselines. The existing ablation (Table 3) is too limited to attribute improvements to the architectural design choices.

Some baseline results are borrowed from other papers (UniMoT) rather than reproduced, making comparisons potentially unreliable due to differences in preprocessing, splits, or hyperparameters. No statistical significance testing is provided for any results.

Zero-shot molecule editing is evaluated only through cherry-picked case studies without systematic quantitative evaluation, making it impossible to assess the generality or reliability of this capability.

Inconsistencies in molecular QA results—Pearson correlations are sometimes lower for MV-CLAM despite better MAE (e.g., molecular weight: r=0.92 vs. 0.98), suggesting potential distribution shift effects that are not discussed.
The paper appears to have truncated sections (abstract and Section 6.5), raising completeness concerns.
review.justification: While MV-CLAM presents a clean and well-motivated architectural contribution (MQ-Former with shared self-attention for multi-view molecular alignment), the experimental evaluation is insufficient to support the paper's claims with confidence. The single-dataset evaluation, missing critical ablations that would isolate the contribution of key design choices (shared attention vs. more tokens, vs. late fusion), borrowed baseline numbers, and qualitative-only assessment of molecule editing collectively fall below the rigor expected at ICLR. The work is a reasonable incremental advance but needs substantially stronger experimental validation.

detailed_results.title: $\texttt{LLaPA}$: Harnessing Language Models for Protein Enzyme Function

review.summary: LLaPA proposes a multimodal LLM framework for predicting Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers by combining protein sequence representations (ESM-2), molecular encoders (ChemBERTa), and a large language model (Vicuna-7B), augmented with a dual-level retrieval engine that retrieves similar proteins and relevant reaction molecules to improve prediction accuracy.
strengths: Creative reformulation of EC number format for autoregressive prediction by replacing period separators with characters distant from digits in embedding space—a transferable insight for structured label prediction with LLMs.
Biologically motivated dual-level retrieval combining protein homology search (mmseq2) and chemical reaction information (Rhea knowledge base) provides meaningful biological context.

Evaluation on three challenging benchmarks including proteins with conflicting annotations (Multi), lesser-known proteins (Halogenase/Price), and novel functions (New), demonstrating breadth of evaluation.
Strong performance on first-three-EC-number prediction (>93% F1 across datasets) suggests the model captures broad enzymatic function categories well.
weaknesses: Fundamental train-test information asymmetry in molecule retrieval: ground truth EC numbers are used to retrieve molecules during training, while retrieved protein EC numbers are used during inference. This introduces information leakage during training and undermines conclusions about the model's functional understanding.

Catastrophic failure without retrieval (near-zero performance on Halogenase and Price when removing SMILES) indicates the LLM has not learned meaningful protein-function mappings and functions primarily as a sophisticated retrieval pipeline.
Low absolute F1 scores on full EC number prediction (0.38, 0.33, 0.44 on key benchmarks) limit practical utility, and BioTranslator outperforms LLaPA on the Multi dataset.

Missing comparisons with recent protein-LLM methods (ProtST, ESM-3, InstructProtein) and erratic baseline behavior (ESM-2 variants with near-zero Acc-2) suggest incomplete or potentially flawed baseline implementations.

The embedding space analysis (t-SNE visualization) supporting the EC format reformulation is superficial—no token-level confusion matrices, perplexity analysis, or rigorous characterization of how tokenization affects prediction quality.
Scalability concerns: requiring Vicuna-7B inference per prediction makes the approach significantly more expensive than CLEAN or fine-tuned ESM-2 methods for proteome-scale annotation.
review.justification: While LLaPA presents some creative ideas (EC format reformulation, dual-level retrieval), it suffers from fundamental methodological flaws: the train-test retrieval asymmetry constitutes information leakage, and the catastrophic failure without retrieval demonstrates the model has not learned genuine protein-function understanding. Combined with low absolute performance, missing key baselines, and questionable baseline implementations, the paper does not meet the acceptance bar for ICLR.

detailed_results.title: Pseudo Physics-Informed Neural Operators

review.summary: PPI-NO proposes learning a 'pseudo physics' surrogate PDE from data using basic differential operators, then coupling it with neural operators via alternating optimization to regularize and improve predictions, especially in data-scarce regimes. The key insight is that PDE locality massively amplifies effective training samples for the physics network, enabling useful physics approximations even from very few operator learning examples.
strengths: The core idea of exploiting PDE locality to amplify training data for learning a physics surrogate is elegant and insightful — a single 128×128 grid pair yields ~16K training points for the PDE network.

Consistent and often dramatic improvements (60-95% error reduction for PPI-FNO) across five diverse benchmarks and two backbone architectures (FNO and DONet), with proper statistical reporting (5 runs with standard deviations).
The fatigue modeling application is compelling and practically relevant — SIF computation lacks a holistic PDE, making standard PINO inapplicable, demonstrating the real-world value of the approach.
The framework is backbone-agnostic and can wrap around any neural operator, enhancing its practical utility and generality.
The paper is clearly written with a well-structured presentation of the alternating optimization scheme and the motivation for pseudo physics.
weaknesses: The assumption that the input distribution p(f) is known (required for the reconstruction loss, Eq. 5) is a significant limitation that substantially undermines the 'minimal physics knowledge' narrative. In many real applications, knowing this distribution may be as challenging as knowing the physics.

No comparison with PINO using the true governing equations, which would establish a critical upper bound and help readers understand how much performance is left on the table with pseudo vs. true physics.

Missing comparisons with other natural baselines: data augmentation strategies, semi-supervised learning approaches, and equation discovery methods (e.g., SINDy-type approaches) that could achieve similar regularization effects.

The selection of which differential operators to include (e.g., {∂x, ∂xx, ∂t, ∂tt, ∂xt}) appears to require non-trivial domain knowledge, partially contradicting the minimal-knowledge premise. No systematic study of how wrong operator choices affect performance.

Lack of theoretical analysis: no formal understanding of when/why pseudo physics helps, no convergence guarantees for the alternating optimization, and no characterization of what properties the pseudo PDE must satisfy to be a useful regularizer.
All benchmarks involve relatively simple 2D PDE systems on regular grids. Scalability to higher dimensions, complex geometries, and truly multi-physics systems remains untested.
review.justification: PPI-NO presents a creative and practically useful idea — learning pseudo physics to regularize neural operators without requiring explicit PDE knowledge — with consistently strong empirical results across multiple benchmarks. However, the theoretical understanding is thin, the 'minimal knowledge' claim is overstated given the need for known input distributions and pre-specified derivative operators, and important baselines (true PINO, data augmentation, equation discovery) are missing. The consistent empirical improvements and the compelling fatigue modeling application tip the balance toward acceptance, but the work would benefit significantly from deeper theoretical analysis and more comprehensive comparisons.

detailed_results.title: CodeBenchGen: Creating Scalable Execution-based Code Generation Benchmarks

review.summary: CodeBenchGen is a framework that uses LLMs (primarily GPT-4) to sandbox arbitrary code fragments from GitHub into self-contained, executable evaluation examples with test cases, enabling scalable creation of code generation benchmarks. The authors demonstrate the framework by producing Exec-CSN, a dataset of 1,931 examples spanning 293 libraries and 367 repositories, validated through realism analysis and a human study.
strengths: Addresses a genuine scalability bottleneck in execution-based benchmark creation—the framework retains 90% of source repositories compared to 5.4% (RepoEval) and 37% (R2E), a compelling practical advantage.

Comprehensive quality verification across four dimensions (diversity, realism, complexity, solvability) with appropriate metrics including BLEU scores, Jaccard similarity on variable names, AST depth correlation, and line coverage analysis.

The human-vs-model analysis reveals an actionable insight: humans dramatically outperform GPT-4 after iterative revision (81.25% vs 57.81%), highlighting a specific weakness in LLM code generation regarding error feedback utilization.
Broad library and repository coverage (293 libraries, 668 topics, 367 repositories) substantially exceeds existing benchmarks like HumanEval (0 external libraries) and SWE-BENCH (12 repositories).
The iterative execution-and-debugging loop is well-designed, with clear progressive improvement shown across iterations (1,260 → 2,343 examples).
Code and data are publicly released, promoting reproducibility.
weaknesses: Circularity concern: GPT-4 is used for benchmark construction (sandboxing and test generation) and then evaluated on the resulting benchmark. The model that created examples may have inherent advantages in solving them, and this issue is not adequately addressed or analyzed.

The 47% conversion rate means over half of input fragments are discarded with no systematic analysis of what types of code fail, limiting understanding of the framework's blind spots and introducing potential selection bias toward 'easier-to-sandbox' code.
The human study is underpowered: only 64 examples (~3.3% of the dataset) evaluated by CS graduate students, which may not generalize to the full dataset or broader developer populations.

The sandboxing process inherently simplifies code by removing real I/O, mocking APIs, and eliminating complex dependencies, meaning the benchmark tests a somewhat artificial version of real-world coding. This tension between executability and realism is not thoroughly discussed.
Only Python is demonstrated; transferability to other languages (especially compiled languages or those with different dependency management) is unknown.
The framework's dependence on GPT-4 makes it expensive and potentially limits accessibility. No experiments explore whether weaker/cheaper models can perform the sandboxing adequately.

LLM-generated tests may miss subtle edge cases; while 95.76% line coverage is reported, semantic coverage and the ability to distinguish correct from subtly incorrect solutions is not rigorously evaluated.
review.justification: The paper addresses a real and timely problem in code generation evaluation with a practical, well-executed framework that achieves substantially broader coverage than existing benchmarks. The realism and diversity analyses are thorough, and the human study, while small, adds meaningful validation. However, the circularity concern (GPT-4 for both construction and evaluation), the lack of failure analysis for the 47% discarded examples, the inherent simplification from sandboxing, and the small human study sample are notable limitations. On balance, the infrastructure contribution and the insights from the human-vs-model comparison provide sufficient value to the community to merit acceptance as a poster.

detailed_results.title: OTGM: Graph Matching with Noisy Correspondence via Optimal Transports

review.summary: The paper proposes OTGM, which reformulates graph matching as a distributional alignment problem using optimal transport, combining Wasserstein distance for node matching and Gromov-Wasserstein distance for edge matching through a unified transport plan, along with a self-supervised graph denoising module to handle noisy edges in input graphs.
strengths: Clean and principled problem formulation that combines node-level (WD) and edge-level (GWD) matching through a shared optimal transport plan, providing a coherent framework for joint coarse- and fine-grained graph matching.

The graph denoising module is well-motivated, with a principled relaxation from intractable ℓ₀ optimization to differentiable optimization using concrete distributions and the reparameterization trick.
Consistent improvements across three standard benchmarks (Pascal VOC, SPair-71k, Willow Object), demonstrating the approach generalizes across different evaluation settings.
A theoretical generalization bound (Theorem 1) is provided, connecting the OT objective and denoising module to error guarantees, adding theoretical grounding to the approach.
The problem of handling noisy correspondences in graph matching is timely and practically relevant, especially as datasets scale and automatic/noisy annotations become more prevalent.
weaknesses: The most critical gap: the paper claims robustness to noisy correspondences but provides no controlled noise experiments where noise is injected at varying rates to demonstrate robustness curves. This is the central claim of the paper yet remains unvalidated.

Quantitative improvements over state-of-the-art are marginal (+1.1% Pascal VOC, +1.2% SPair-71k, +0.5% Willow Object), and the Willow Object dataset is nearly saturated at 99.6%, limiting the ability to demonstrate meaningful differences.

The generalization bound in Theorem 1 appears to be a relatively standard adaptation of existing OT domain adaptation bounds rather than providing deep new theoretical insights specific to graph matching.

Ablation study (Figure 4) lacks depth: no detailed numerical breakdowns, no error bars or confidence intervals, insufficient exploration of sensitivity to the λ parameter balancing WD and GWD, and no analysis of what the denoising module's learned masks actually capture.
Missing computational cost analysis — OT solvers can be expensive, and the paper does not discuss scalability or runtime comparisons with baselines.

Key algorithmic details (Algorithm 1, OT solver specifics, convergence properties) are relegated to the appendix, making the main text insufficient for assessing the full methodology. No code availability is mentioned, raising reproducibility concerns.

Several components (InfoNCE contrastive loss from CLIP, concrete distribution reparameterization) are borrowed from prior work with limited adaptation, reducing the methodological novelty of individual components.
review.justification: While the paper presents a principled formulation combining optimal transport with graph denoising for robust graph matching, it fails to validate its central claim of noise robustness through controlled experiments with varying noise levels. The quantitative improvements are incremental, the theoretical contribution is a standard adaptation of existing OT bounds, and the experimental evaluation lacks sufficient depth (no error bars, incomplete ablations, no computational analysis). The gap between the paper's claims about handling noisy correspondences and its experimental evidence is too large for acceptance.

detailed_results.title: Understanding Synthetic Context Extension via Retrieval Heads

review.summary: This paper investigates why synthetic context extension works (or fails) for long-context LLMs by using retrieval heads as a mechanistic interpretability lens. The authors systematically vary synthetic data along concept expression and context diversity axes, finding that retrieval head overlap between synthetic- and real-data-trained models correlates with downstream performance, and that these heads are necessary but not sufficient for good performance.
strengths: Novel analytical framework that bridges synthetic data engineering and mechanistic interpretability using retrieval heads as a diagnostic tool, opening a promising research direction

Systematic experimental design with a principled concept expression × context diversity grid, three qualitatively different retrieval tasks (MDQA, MuSiQue, SummHay Citation), and two base models providing converging evidence

Multiple complementary analysis techniques (retrieval score computation, attention knockout, activation patching, correlation analysis) all pointing toward consistent conclusions, strengthening the claims
Useful empirical correction to prior work (Xiong et al., 2024): symbolic data does not outperform real data at 32K context length, contradicting claims made at shorter contexts
The cross-task retrieval head analysis (Table 2) showing task-specific head subsets adds meaningful nuance to the retrieval heads literature
weaknesses: Limited model scale (only 7-8B parameter models) and modest context extension (8K→32K) raise questions about generalizability to larger models and much longer contexts (64K+) where the practical need is greatest

The paper diagnoses why synthetic data fails but offers limited concrete, actionable guidance for creating better synthetic data—the gap between understanding and prescription significantly limits practical impact

The 'necessary but not sufficient' finding, while honest, weakens the explanatory power: high retrieval head overlap does not guarantee good performance, and the Spearman correlations vary substantially across tasks and models (e.g., weak for Mistral on MDQA)
Activation patching improvements are modest in absolute terms and sometimes negative (MuSiQue High-High), limiting the strength of the sufficiency argument

No comparison with alternative interpretability lenses (e.g., probing classifiers, information flow analysis) to argue that retrieval heads are the most informative diagnostic for this specific analysis
review.justification: The paper presents a creative and well-executed interpretability study that connects synthetic data quality to retrieval head activation patterns, providing mechanistic explanations for an empirically observed phenomenon. The systematic experimental design and converging evidence from multiple analytical techniques are strong. However, the limited model scale, modest context lengths, variable correlation strengths, and the significant gap between diagnosis and prescription temper the significance. It is a solid contribution that meets the acceptance bar but represents an incremental rather than transformative advance.

detailed_results.title: OT-Attack: Enhancing Adversarial Transferability of Vision-Language Models via Optimal Transport Optimization

review.summary: The paper proposes OT-Attack, which applies optimal transport theory to improve adversarial transferability of Vision-Language Pre-trained (VLP) models by computing optimal alignments between data-augmented image sets and corresponding text sets, replacing the naive average alignment used in prior work (SGA).
strengths: Well-motivated observation that augmented image crops naturally align better with certain captions than others, making naive averaging suboptimal
Principled mathematical formulation using optimal transport with efficient Sinkhorn algorithm computation
Comprehensive experimental setup covering four VLP models (ALBEF, TCL, CLIP ViT, CLIP CNN), two datasets (Flickr30K, MSCOCO), and multiple baselines
Consistent improvements in black-box transfer settings, with some notable gains (e.g., 7.4% improvement on ALBEF→CLIP CNN)
Thorough hyperparameter sensitivity analysis across caption quantity, scale quantity, and perturbation strength
weaknesses: The contribution is narrowly incremental over SGA—essentially replacing mean alignment with OT-based alignment while keeping the rest of the framework largely intact

No comparison with alternative alignment strategies (Hungarian matching, attention-weighted alignment, top-k selection) to justify why OT is specifically necessary rather than any non-uniform alignment
The claim that reduced white-box performance indicates less overfitting lacks rigorous analysis (e.g., gradient diversity analysis, loss landscape visualization, or formal overfitting metrics)
Cross-task transferability improvements (Tables 6 and 7) are marginal—often within 0.2-0.7 points of SGA—undermining practical significance claims
Sinkhorn hyperparameters (λ=0.1, convergence threshold) are fixed without sensitivity analysis or theoretical justification
Claims about attacking commercial models (GPT-4, Bing Chat) appear qualitative and lack systematic evaluation
review.justification: While the paper presents a clean and principled application of optimal transport to address a genuine limitation of SGA's naive averaging, the contribution is fundamentally incremental—a surgical replacement of one alignment mechanism with another. The absence of comparison with alternative alignment strategies (which could achieve similar gains) weakens the claim that OT is specifically needed, and cross-task improvements are marginal. The paper would benefit significantly from deeper mechanistic analysis of why OT reduces overfitting and from demonstrating that simpler alignment alternatives cannot match its performance.

detailed_results.title: CodeUpdateArena: Benchmarking Knowledge Editing on API Updates

review.summary: The paper introduces CodeUpdateArena, a benchmark for evaluating knowledge editing in the code domain, specifically targeting API function updates. It consists of 670 program synthesis examples covering 161 synthetic updates to 54 functions across 7 Python packages, and experiments reveal that fine-tuning on documentation alone is insufficient while fine-tuning on usage examples shows improvement but at the cost of specificity.
strengths: Identifies and formalizes an important, practical problem at the intersection of knowledge editing and code LLMs that has not been previously studied
Well-designed UPass@k evaluation metric that verifies genuine use of updates by requiring solutions to pass with updated API and fail with the original, preventing superficial shortcuts
Careful multi-stage data generation pipeline with multiple validation checkpoints (unit test verification, manual filtering, literalization of test answers)

Comprehensive experimental design spanning proprietary vs. open-source models, prepending vs. fine-tuning, documentation vs. examples, with informative ablation studies on training data composition and learning rate sensitivity
The key finding that documentation-based fine-tuning fails while example-based fine-tuning succeeds is practically significant and actionable for API providers and model developers
Thoughtful use of synthetic updates to avoid data contamination from future pre-training corpora
weaknesses: Existing knowledge editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, MEND) cannot be directly evaluated on this benchmark since they target different model architectures or input-output formats, limiting the paper's ability to assess the state of the art
Specificity evaluation is narrow: only 82 HumanEval instances across 25 random edits, which may not reliably capture catastrophic forgetting patterns
Only single-edit scenarios are evaluated; no sequential or batch editing experiments, which is the more realistic and challenging setting
The efficacy-specificity tradeoff remains largely unsolved—the best FT(PS) approach still causes significant specificity degradation (up to 30-point drops in SPass@k)
Moderate benchmark scale (161 updates, 670 examples) covering only Python and 7 packages limits generalizability claims
Heavy reliance on GPT-4 for both data generation and as an evaluation baseline creates potential circularity concerns, and threshold choices for quality filtering (70%, 60%) appear somewhat arbitrary
Cross-validation with only ~3-5 examples per update is constrained and may lead to high variance in FT(PS) results
review.justification: The paper fills a genuine gap by creating the first benchmark for knowledge editing in the code domain, with a thoughtful evaluation design (UPass@k) and informative experimental findings. The key insight that code knowledge editing requires semantic reasoning rather than syntactic recall elevates this beyond a simple benchmark contribution. However, the inability to evaluate existing knowledge editing methods, the narrow specificity evaluation, the unsolved efficacy-specificity tradeoff, and moderate scale temper enthusiasm. It is a solid contribution that should catalyze future research, meeting the acceptance bar as a poster.

detailed_results.title: In-Context Neural PDE: Learning to Adapt a Neural Solver to Different Physics

review.summary: The paper introduces IC-NPDE, a framework that uses a large transformer-based hypernetwork to process context states and generate parameters for a smaller CNN-based neural ODE solver, decoupling parameter estimation from state prediction for unknown temporal PDEs. This mirrors classical numerical methods while preserving in-context learning capabilities, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and generalization over transformer baselines.
strengths: Clean and well-motivated architectural design that draws principled connections between finite difference methods, CNNs, and neural ODEs, providing a natural inductive bias for temporal PDE problems.

The information bottleneck created by mapping a large hypernetwork to a small solver is elegant, and the UMAP analysis convincingly shows the hypernetwork clusters contexts by underlying PDE parameters rather than initial conditions.
Dramatic sample efficiency improvements (1% error in 1 epoch vs. 50 for baseline) have significant practical implications for data-scarce scientific applications.
Comprehensive evaluation across seven diverse PDE datasets spanning 1D and 2D problems with varying equation types, boundary conditions, and coefficients.
Multi-physics pretraining results showing improved performance over single-dataset training align well with foundation model aspirations for scientific computing.
The 3× parameter reduction compared to the primary baseline while achieving superior performance demonstrates the efficiency of the approach.
weaknesses: The primary comparison is against a single ICL baseline (A ViT), with limited comparison to other recent ICL-for-PDE methods (Yang et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Yang & Osher, 2024). The fine-tuning comparisons with U-Net, FNO, and AR-diffusion are against non-ICL methods, making the comparison uneven.
No computational cost analysis (wall-clock training/inference time) is provided. The adjoint method with 30 Runge-Kutta integration steps likely adds significant overhead that should be benchmarked.
Restriction to uniform grids is a major limitation for real-world applicability, particularly for complex geometries common in engineering and scientific applications.
The kernel size requires manual adjustment across problems (k=5 vs k=11 for compressible NS), suggesting problem-specific tuning that undermines the goal of a general-purpose solver.
Multi-step rollout errors accumulate substantially at longer horizons (t+32), and IC-NPDE doesn't consistently dominate (e.g., shearflow in Tab. 5), raising questions about long-term stability.
The framework assumes time-independent dynamics; extension to time-dependent PDEs is mentioned but unexplored. Sensitivity to context length T is also not analyzed.
review.justification: The paper presents a conceptually clean and well-motivated contribution that elegantly decouples parameter estimation from state prediction, mirroring classical numerical methods. The sample efficiency gains and multi-physics generalization are compelling. However, the limited baseline comparisons (primarily one ICL method), absence of computational cost analysis, restriction to uniform grids, and need for manual kernel size tuning temper the impact. The contribution is solid enough for acceptance but falls short of a stronger recommendation due to these experimental gaps.

detailed_results.title: Criteria and Bias of Parameterized Linear Regression under Edge of Stability Regime

review.summary: This paper demonstrates that the Edge of Stability (EoS) phenomenon can occur with quadratic loss functions under diagonal linear network parameterization (β = w²₊ - w²₋), challenging the prevailing belief that subquadratic loss growth is necessary. The authors provide empirical evidence and non-asymptotic convergence analysis in two step-size regimes (ημ < 1 and ημ > 1), characterizing distinct implicit bias properties in each.
strengths: The core finding—that EoS occurs under quadratic loss through reparameterization—is genuinely novel and challenges conventional wisdom in the field, redirecting attention from loss function properties to parameterization structure.

The phase transition proof technique for the ημ > 1 regime, involving time-varying dynamical systems transitioning from envelope-expanding to damped oscillation, is technically creative and extends beyond prior methods.

Comprehensive and systematic empirical study with well-designed ablation experiments testing each condition's necessity (d ≥ 2, y ≠ 0, non-degenerate features), providing a thorough empirical picture.

The characterization of distinct implicit bias properties under the two step-size regimes—sparse recovery for ημ < 1 vs. non-vanishing error for ημ > 1—provides practically relevant insights about step-size selection in diagonal linear networks.
The multi-sample empirical extension suggesting overparameterization (d > n) is necessary for EoS with quadratic loss is an interesting observation that could guide future work.
weaknesses: The theoretical setting is extremely restrictive: d = 2, 1-sparse prior, single data point. While the authors claim qualitative behavior generalizes across dimensions, this is not proven, severely limiting the scope of the theoretical results.

Critical assumptions in the main theorems are circular or incomplete: Theorem 1 assumes the change-of-sign condition (r_t · r_{t+1} < 0) rather than deriving it, and Proposition 1 essentially assumes convergence (excluding divergence and chaos) to prove convergence.
The upper bound on ημ of approximately 1.12 in Theorem 2 is acknowledged as artificial, meaning the analysis only covers a narrow sliver of the EoS regime beyond ημ = 1.

The underlying mechanism (non-vanishing third-order derivatives from reparameterization) is acknowledged to be consistent with existing theoretical frameworks (Damian et al., 2022), somewhat reducing the conceptual surprise of the main finding.

The multi-sample analysis—arguably the most impactful generalization—remains purely empirical with no theoretical backing, and the gap between this simplified model and actual neural network training dynamics is substantial.
review.justification: While the paper presents a genuinely novel observation that EoS can occur with quadratic loss through reparameterization, the theoretical analysis has significant gaps: circular assumptions in key theorems, an extremely restricted setting (d=2, single sample, 1-sparse prior), and a narrow valid range for the step-size parameter. The observation itself is interesting but the underlying mechanism aligns with existing frameworks, and the most impactful generalization (multi-sample) lacks theoretical support. The contribution is meaningful but incremental, falling slightly below the acceptance threshold.

detailed_results.title: Does Instruction Tuning Reduce Diversity? A Case Study Using Code Generation

review.summary: This paper investigates whether instruction tuning reduces the diversity of LLM outputs by leveraging code generation as a testbed, where executable semantics enable objective measurement of semantic diversity. The key finding is that while instruction tuning reduces lexical and syntactic diversity, it can actually increase semantic diversity, and that neural diversity metrics correlate poorly with semantic diversity.
strengths: Elegant methodological contribution: using code's executable semantics to objectively measure semantic diversity, cleanly separating lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity levels

Comprehensive experimental design spanning multiple model families (Llama-3, CodeLlama, Qwen-Coder, commercial models), sizes, prompting strategies, and instruction-tuning methods (SFT, PPO, DPO, rejection sampling)
Strong statistical practices including non-parametric tests (Wilcoxon signed-rank), effect sizes (Cohen's d), and proof of sample-size invariance for the pairwise diversity metric

The counterintuitive finding that instruction tuning increases semantic diversity while decreasing surface diversity is important and well-supported, reframing the 'mode collapse' narrative around RLHF
The demonstration that neural diversity metrics (CodeBERTScore, ICE-Score) correlate negatively with semantic diversity is a striking cautionary finding for the community
weaknesses: The dataset comprises only 21 problems from competitive programming, which is small and drawn from a narrow domain, raising concerns about generalizability of the findings

Generalizability beyond code is the central limitation—code has deterministic, verifiable semantics while natural language does not, making the leap from these findings to broader conclusions about instruction tuning uncertain
No mechanistic analysis of why instruction tuning increases semantic diversity; the paper offers speculation but no investigation into the underlying cause
Missing important ablations: temperature-adjusted comparisons between base and instruction-tuned models to control for coherence rate differences would strengthen causal claims
The semantic diversity metric is binary (outputs match or don't across test cases), failing to capture degrees of semantic difference between programs
SFT-only results are weak and inconsistent (Table 1), making it difficult to cleanly attribute the semantic diversity increase to preference tuning versus SFT or total training
review.justification: The paper presents a clever and well-executed methodology for studying diversity through code's executable semantics, yielding a counterintuitive and important finding that instruction tuning can increase semantic diversity despite reducing surface-level diversity. The critique of neural diversity metrics is also valuable. However, the small dataset (21 problems), limited generalizability beyond code, lack of mechanistic explanation, and missing temperature-controlled ablations prevent this from being a strong contribution. It is a solid case study that should stimulate more rigorous thinking about diversity measurement, meeting the acceptance bar as a poster.

detailed_results.title: Learnability of Discrete Dynamical Systems under High Classification Noise

review.summary: This paper investigates learning discrete dynamical systems (threshold-based systems on networks) from noisy training data under the PAC model, presenting two algorithms (V-ERM and VisRange) with provable sample complexity bounds that nearly match noise-free upper bounds, demonstrating that noise only introduces a constant factor overhead independent of system size.
strengths: The 'noise comes free' result is surprising and practically important: noise adds only a constant factor (depending on η, not n) to sample complexity, matching the noise-free information-theoretic upper bound
Clean theoretical framework with a well-structured layered algorithm design (V-ERM → VisScore → VisRange) that is both pedagogically effective and technically sound
Near-optimal bounds with only an O(log n) gap between upper and lower bounds, demonstrating tightness of the analysis
Non-trivial extension of Random Classification Noise to multi-class structured settings where almost all training labels are corrupted (probability of fully correct label → 0 as n grows)
The vertex-wise decomposition approach elegantly addresses computational hardness of standard ERM while enabling parallelization
Well-motivated problem: learning cascading dynamics from noisy observations fills a genuine gap in the literature with clear applications to epidemiology, social media, and financial contagion
weaknesses: Restricted to threshold functions only—no discussion of extension to other interaction function classes (symmetric, monotone, or general Boolean functions), which limits the broader applicability
Known graph assumption is restrictive in practice where network topology may also need inference; while theoretically justified by impossibility results, this narrows the practical scope
Uniform noise rate assumption (all bounded by η̄ < 1/2) excludes adversarial or heterogeneous noise models that may be more realistic in some applications
Experimental evaluation appears incomplete based on the review materials—difficult to fully assess empirical claims about phase transitions and practical performance
